data.title: HS2 in turmoil after steel beam hits cage carrying two workers
data.description:
HS2 has been left in turmoil after the cage that two workers were standing inside was hit by a steel beam during construction work in London.

The workers, who were inside a cherrypicker’s cage, narrowly escaped being crushed by a steel beam swinging across a tunnel ventilation shaft, insiders said.

All HS2 construction work in London has now been halted pending a review of safety procedures, the company confirmed.

Everything except for essential maintenance has been “brought to a safe stop” as a result of the safety incident, an HS2 spokesman said.

A full shutdown of London construction will not only add to those delays but damage even further the railway line’s already-dented reputation.

The incident took place on September 29, when a gantry crane was being dismantled at the Green Park Way ventilation shaft in west London.

Four giant tunnel boring machines (TBMs) are currently being dismantled there and removed from the tunnels.

A steel beam from a crane being used to dismantle one of the TBMs struck a cherrypicker basket containing two workers at height.

Although nobody was injured, the incident posed a “serious risk to those involved”, HS2 acknowledged.

Halt comes amid ongoing ‘comprehensive reset’

Works across London are being carried out by a joint venture company called Skanska Costain STRABAG, which decided to stop work after discussions with HS2 bosses.

News of the halt to building works comes amid an ongoing “comprehensive reset” of the repeatedly delayed and over-budget project by Mark Wild, its chief executive.

Mr Wild has been tasked by the Labour Government to break HS2’s “cycle of cost increases and delays”.

He is expected to report back to Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, with recommendations on how to achieve that, early next year.

In a report completed shortly after he was appointed HS2’s chief executive last December, Mr Wild also indicated that the project will not be completed until between 2036 and 2039.

The HS2 boss told the Department for Transport (DfT) that the cost of completing HS2 has soared to £81bn in 2019 prices, Rail magazine reported.

With inflation taken into account, taxpayers will be picking up a final bill widely expected to exceed £100bn for the new railway line.

In its current form, HS2 will link London to Birmingham, shaving about ten minutes off the current journey time.

Originally, HS2 would have stretched well into northern England, but a series of cuts to the project thanks to vast cost overruns have seen its scope drastically reduced.

The project was originally conceived to add extra rail capacity in parallel to the West Coast Main Line, which has no space for future expansion of rail services.

Mr Wild told The Telegraph: “Safety is our number one priority at HS2. Due to a serious incident, works have been safely halted across the London tunnels section of the route.

“While no workers were physically harmed, our contractor, Skanska Costain STRABAG joint venture, is undertaking a comprehensive review of safety procedures and leadership on their sites before a safe restart can take place.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Europe’s carmakers became hooked on Chinese chips. Now they can’t get them
data.description:
The modern car is made up of thousands of components. But a shortage of just one can be enough to bring production to a halt.

On Wednesday, German automotive giant Volkswagen (VW) warned workers of potential stoppages in the coming weeks as it grapples with a sudden shortage of Chinese-made microchips.

And it’s not just VW. The issue is threatening to engulf nearly all carmakers as nearly all of them depend on the same Chinese chips, one way or another, the European Automotive Manufacturers’ Association (ACEA) has warned.

The cause of the fuss? A bitter row between the European and Chinese divisions of major semiconductor company Nexperia, which has brought the flow of chips to a halt.

The bust up follows a decision by the government of the Netherlands to remove Nexperia’s Chinese leadership and take control of the company last week amid concerns about national security.

In response, Chinese staff at Nexperia were told to ignore instructions from the group’s Dutch head office and China has curbed shipments of the vital chips to Europe.

This is a major problem, because Nexperia, which is owned by Jiaxing-based Wingtech, makes chips in Europe but then sends them to China to be finished and packaged.

Overall, Nexperia accounts for around two fifths of the transistors and diodes used by the automotive industry, according to research company TechInsights.

But even this large figure underplays the significance of its chips, which are relatively simple but ubiquitous in the electronics of modern vehicles.

Carmakers do not buy them directly. Instead, the chips are part of printed circuit boards (PCBs) that go into hardware for systems for electronic windows, seat adjustment mechanisms or many other electric components such as knobs and dials on the dashboard.

“It could almost be any of the electronics in the car,” explains Ian Riches, of TechInsights.

“But the car manufacturers themselves have very limited visibility as to how exposed they are, because they’re not purchasing these chips directly.

“And these are parts that sell for cents, not dollars. So Nexperia ships huge volumes of them but they’re relatively low value.

“That tends to mean it is the sort of thing you don’t think about until it goes wrong.”

‘War rooms’

The fact things had gone badly wrong quickly became clear on October 10 when Nexperia notified customers that it was “no longer able to guarantee delivery of their chips”.

Since then, carmakers have set up “war rooms” to find potential solutions and are urging the Hague and Beijing to find a diplomatic solution urgently.

For the automotive industry it is yet another unwelcome crisis in a year where the sector has also been battered by Donald Trump’s imposition of trade tariffs and China has threatened to choke off supplies of rare earth magnets that are also used for cars.

In Britain, the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders has warned of severe consequences if the chips don’t start to flow again.

“Another semiconductor chip shortage is the last thing the global automotive industry needs. While the sector has made efforts to diversify its supply chains, if not resolved quickly, this issue has the potential to severely disrupt vehicle production and market supply,” it said.

The Nexperia row has echoes of the chip shortage that struck carmakers during the Covid pandemic, when suddenly companies found they couldn’t get hold of supplies fast enough as the economy reopened.

“The auto industry shut down, and cars weren’t getting made, and so the auto industry turned around to its semiconductor partners and said ‘we don’t need your stuff for a while’,” says Riches.

“Then everyone went home and started buying laptops and games consoles and certain areas of the semiconductor industry absolutely boomed, so capacity got switched over to non-automotive lines.”

After automotive companies suddenly started demanding chips, the ensuing shortage forced temporary shutdowns.

But this shortage is different, because it isn’t self-inflicted. Instead, it is a reminder that sourcing components from China is more risky than it used to be.

Nexperia’s halt on exports could damage the Chinese car industry as well as Europe’s, argues Riches, but the damage will be more pronounced in the Continent.

Andrew Bergbaum, an automotive expert at consultancy AlixPartners, said automotive groups were battling to find alternatives.

“Car companies are standing up the chip war rooms that they created during the pandemic,” he says. “You only need one component to be unique to Nexperia and that means that the whole PCB can’t be delivered.

“What they do know, however, is that once you take out a huge amount of capacity from the market, regardless of whether it’s interchangeable or not, the whole supply chain enters a bullwhip.”

Should the dispute drag on, design changes to remove the Nexperia chips are possible. Car companies argue they already tried to mitigate supply chain risks following the pandemic chip shortages.

Preparing for geopolitics

Many are also increasingly stockpiling certain components as well to try to mitigate supply shocks.

Swapping out Chinese components entirely is possible but – in an industry that prides itself on being global and highly efficient – that could end up making cars everywhere more expensive.

“Carmakers are having to come up with more local supply chains and are moving from ‘just in time’ to ‘just in case’,” says Bergbaum.

Mr Bergbaum added: “The implications of that are massive. The speed that the companies can go at depends on the refresh cycle of the cars they bring to market, but undoubtedly as you take away those economies of scale and localise things, by definition, you are making it less efficient.”

Still, carmakers are now becoming increasingly prepared for geopolitics to become a bigger risk to production.

The Dutch takeover of Nexperia’s operations reportedly came after US authorities raised concerns about Zhang Xuezheng, the company’s chief executive, remaining in post.

Soon afterwards, the Hague invoked Cold War-era laws citing “major shortcomings that could jeopardise security of supply” of microchips to Europe.

Dutch and Chinese officials have since held talks in a bid to end the standoff. But Beijing’s officials were not in a conciliatory mood, accusing their European counterparts of overstretching the definition of national security. 

“Measures taken by the Dutch side regarding Nexperia Semiconductor have seriously affected the stability of global industrial and supply chains,” the Chinese commerce ministry said in its statement.

Now, VW’s warning to staff suggests automotive bosses fear the crisis is far from being resolved.

“I think this could be a short crunch, and hopefully wiser heads will prevail,” says Riches at TechInsights.

“But there are bigger games at play here than just whether a car gets made or not. And that’s the danger – if it gets caught up in something larger, maybe it could drag on.

“In the Covid crisis, it truly was structural and even with the best will in the world, they just couldn’t turn the taps back on again. This is entirely political, and the taps could be turned back on tomorrow with the stroke of a pen.”

For now, it means geopolitics is just as crucial to the modern car as the microchip.

data.title: AI says the King is right on architecture
data.description:
For years, critics mocked the King’s insistence that buildings should feel humane with legible streets, rhythm, depth and materials that encourage care, not contempt. A new study I co-authored with Professor Nikos Salingaros put the King’s common-sense view to a simple test. We asked artificial intelligence to judge architecture the way people do. It did, and sided with the public, not with fashionable orthodoxy.

Our method was disarmingly plain. We showed large language models including ChatGPT paired images where independent Deltapoll surveys had already shown public preferences: Bath Rugby’s stadium, an HS2 viaduct and Oxford Street’s Marks & Spencer. We then ran three tests: emotional criteria (beauty, calm, coherence, comfort, reassurance); geometrical criteria from Christopher Alexander’s ‘living geometry’ (levels of scale, strong centres, local symmetry, positive space, alternating repetition); and a simple question: which design feels friendlier?

In every case, the models preferred the more human-centred option, matching the independent polling. At Orchard House, where Marks & Spencer wish to demolish a delightful 1930s classical building and replace it with heartless, spreadsheet modernism, the AI consistently chose a traditionally composed alternative by Francis Terry and Create Streets. The public poll showed 79 per cent support for the humane option.

The AI’s emotional and geometrical tests were, if anything, more emphatic. Bath told the same story: the people favoured the classical stone stadium. AI agreed. On the HS2 viaduct, the public preferred the design with bricks and detail. The AI again chose the same. Across repeated runs and different AI systems, the results held. Some 97 per cent of the first two tests and 100 per cent of the test for friendliness agreed with the people. Human-centred design correlates with positive emotions and positive emotions correlate with public preference.

Why does this matter? Because design too often confuses novelty with progress. We have spent a century perfecting an aesthetic that treats people as throughput: over-scaled flatness; endless glass; object-buildings that ignore the street; visual cacophony not public courtesy. The result is places that many quietly dislike but feel powerless to resist. Our findings suggest two uncomfortable truths for the design establishment and one practical route forward.

First, the public are not philistines. Given a clear choice, they pick the design that looks as if it cares for them: façades with depth and pattern; materials that age gracefully; forms that acknowledge human scale; bridges that enrich the landscape rather than besmirch it. Asked to “think with empathy”, the AI picks the same.

Second, what is waved away as “nostalgia” is better understood as “neuro-aesthetics”. The eye and brain reward legible order and patterned complexity. That is why “living geometry” (layers of scale, repetition with variation, strong centres) keeps appearing in the buildings people love and the streets where they linger. Our tests captured that link.

And the route forward? Make this normal. If we want faster delivery, we must code for the qualities people prefer instead of relying on elite opinion and opaque processes. That means simple, visual design codes for frontages, plots and materials. It means using tools, including AI, to diagnose friendliness early, not to generate ever wilder images. It means treating human preference as the objective, not an optional garnish.

Does AI settle every argument about architecture? Of course not. But it does something valuable. It acts as an impartial mirror, reflecting the emotional reality of our neighbourhoods. Ask it to judge like a human and it prefers the human-centred design. In that sense the King is right and importantly so. Buildings that look after us help us to feel looked after. Build more of them and we would be more prosperous, content and civilised.

Nicholas Boys Smith is the founding Chairman of Create Streets

data.title: Nicholas Sibley, larger-than-life banker and senior figure in the Hong Kong financial scene
data.description:
Nicholas Sibley, who has died aged 87, was a bon vivant financier in the last hurrah of Hong Kong’s British-dominated era.

As managing director of Jardine Fleming, Hong Kong’s leading merchant banking and stockbroking firm, and later as regional chairman of Barclays’ investment banking interests, Nick Sibley was described as “an A-list celebrity of the Asian financial community”, in which he was a byword for sharp wit and tireless conviviality as well as dealmaking.

An accountant by training, he began a City career with Robert Fleming & Co, the merchant bank which in 1970 joint-ventured in Hong Kong with the trading house of Jardine Matheson to form Jardine Fleming. Sibley was seconded in 1972 in time to advise Hong Kong Land (the property arm of Jardines) in a takeover of the Dairy Farm chain of supermarkets and restaurants.

The £100 million hostile bid was a first in Hong Kong’s small and interwoven corporate arena. Jardine Fleming was about to venture from its home territory into all the emerging markets of the region, and many more deals followed, often involving Sibley’s team as in-house advisers to Jardine group companies.

One such in 1976 saw complex manoeuvres around a possible takeover of the rival hong (trading house) of Wheelock Marden; it was defended by Wardley, a HongKong Bank subsidiary which was Jardine Fleming’s lesser local rival.

Legend had it that when asked at a cocktail party whether he knew any merchant banking jokes, Sibley’s one-word reply was “Wardleys”. Such bon mots were to be heard in his regular Any Questions-style slot on Radio Hong Kong and at lunchtime at his Hong Kong Club table. Evenings in the shark’s fin eateries and high-class watering-holes of Wanchai were likewise full of merriment, Sibley the centre of attention among a coterie of expat and Chinese market players.

But he was also a shrewd judge of market behaviour: perhaps his best-known dictum, much repeated during the 2008 financial crisis, was recorded by the Spectator columnist Christopher Fildes as Sibley’s Law: “Giving capital to a bank is like giving a gallon of beer to a drunk. You know what will come of it but you can’t know which wall he will choose.”

Nicholas Theobald Sibley was born in London on June 20 1938, to Gilbert Sibley, an accountant and company director, and his wife Katherine, née Mathew, who came from a notable family of Catholic lawyers and priests. Her father, Sir Theobald Mathew (1898-1964), was director of public prosecutions, and her Irish three-times-great uncle was the Capuchin friar Theobald Mathew (1790-1856), an ardent teetotalist known as the Apostle of Abstinence, whose statues stand in Dublin and Cork.

Young Nicholas (Nick or Nicky to his intimates) was educated at Downside and enjoyed National Service with the Queen’s Royal Irish Hussars, but stayed only briefly at Trinity College, Oxford, until he was rusticated and chose not to return. Instead, he followed his father to qualify as a chartered accountant with Price Waterhouse, before joining Robert Fleming.

At Jardine Fleming he became deputy managing director in 1978 and was managing director from 1979 to 1983, when he returned as a director of Fleming in London. Compared to the buzz of Hong Kong he found the City “stodgy”, as one colleague put it – and, according to another, “himself no longer the biggest fish in the pond”.

But a more exciting prospect loomed amid the 1986 “Big Bang” reforms which created ambitious new trading firms. Sibley was hired as head of international equities for Barclays de Zoete Wedd (BZW), the fledgling investment bank whose parental supply of capital was a match for his expansive ambitions.

A multiplicity of stockbroking and corporate-finance outposts were rapidly acquired from Sydney to Paris, on the not-always-reliable principle of buying like-minded businesses and leaving them to get on with it. “They know the sort of thing,” was a favourite Sibley catchphrase.

In 1989, he returned to Hong Kong in a more presidential role, overseeing BZW activities throughout the Asia-Pacific region. But when the group’s internal politics became increasingly uncongenial, he moved again in 1993 to lead a short-lived joint venture between NatWest and the Wheelock real estate group, by then owned by the family of the shipping tycoon Sir YK Pao.

Thereafter Sibley focused on non-executive roles and private investments, ranging from shipping in Hong Kong and minerals in Africa to timberland in the Philippines – where he also found himself called upon to judge a beauty contest. Among his private passions was a collection of vintage Rolls-Royces, one garaged in Japan for his regular visits there.

A committed trencherman and oenophile, and a hugely generous host, Sibley kept splendid cellars at all his homes, which included a watermill in the south of France once owned by the novelist Marcel Pagnol and a contemporary sea-view villa in New Zealand. But he also had the willpower (and the persuasive force of his wife, Sally) to shed weight from time to time, and in his later years to follow his Irish forebear’s abstinent light.

Sally, whom he married in 1965, was in fact his wine merchant’s daughter: her father, Keith Stevens, was chairman of Corney & Barrow, where Nick Sibley became a long-serving board member. The firm’s current chairman described him as “a great sharer, not just of wine but of experiences, challenges and initiatives; with a quicksilver mind that positively crackled with energy.”

Sally died in 2021, and Nick Sibley is survived by their daughter and son.

Nicholas Sibley, born June 20 1938, died September 23 2025

data.title: Energy bosses attack regulator amid backlash over bills
data.description:
Energy bosses have urged Labour to break up Ofgem as it accused the industry regulator of “pushing up costs for the very customers it tries to protect”.

The boss of Energy UK, the industry body representing suppliers including Octopus and British Gas owner Centrica, claimed Ofgem’s failures were a key cause of surging customer debts, unstable energy markets and a failure to generate investment.

Dhara Vyas accused the regulator of taking a “bureaucratic and onerous approach” that “result[s] in a heavy regulatory burden that does little for customers, driving up costs, and actively diverting the sector from efforts to innovate”.

She called for the quango to be broken up, with its workforce cut back and many of its functions taken away. It is the first time Energy UK has launched such an attack on the regulator.

Rachel Reeves has vowed to rein in watchdogs in an attempt to boost Britain’s economy. The Chancellor has pledged to scrap reams of red tape in an effort to encourage the Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR) to take a more positive view of the economy.

Writing in The Telegraph, Ms Vyas said: “There is a growing sense, in our sector and elsewhere, that the energy regulator has lost its way.

“By becoming too big and bureaucratic, it has overseen a dramatic increase in red tape, reducing growth and pushing up costs for the very customers it tries to protect.

“Breaking up Ofgem – with its consumer protection and competition role moving to the Competition and Markets Authority, and scheme delivery oversight shifting to other suitable organisations – will allow it to concentrate on the right priorities.”

The intervention is highly significant given Energy UK’s standing. Its membership includes around 80 companies involved in electricity and gas supply, including Drax, Tesla and France’s state power company EDF, which runs the UK’s nuclear plants.

Ed Miliband is already reviewing Ofgem’s functions under an audit of the regulator launched last December. Recommendations for change are said to be imminent and Ms Vyas said the Energy Secretary should “radically reduce Ofgem’s duties”.

Ms Vyas blamed Ofgem for failing to spot problems in the sector in the run up to the 2022 energy crisis and said “knee-jerk” reactions since then had pushed up energy costs. This has fuelled a sharp rise in unpaid bills, with energy debt now standing at a record £4.5bn.

It follows last week’s criticisms of government energy policy by senior executives from Octopus Energy, British Gas owner Centrica, E.On and EDF. MPs were warned that household bills were on course to rise by hundreds of pounds this decade because of ballooning green levies.

Mo Jamei, the director of economic policy at Confederation of British Industry (CBI), warned MPs on Tuesday that Britain’s high energy costs were also a growing burden on businesses and said government policy was fuelling the problem.

He told the business and trade select committee: “Policy costs are such a big proportion of our total electricity costs in the UK – up to 24pc. That is the area the Government could make a significant intervention on, either making them repayable over a long period or offsetting them for a number of years.

“There are no good solutions in this space and we know that more policy costs are coming down the track in the next five years for transmission, for distribution, and to pay for nuclear and other energy sources but we can not continually add these to the bills of businesses. It will make us more uncompetitive than we are at the moment.”

An Ofgem spokesman said: “We will continue to work closely with the industry, its trade body Energy UK, and consumer groups as the energy system evolves, and the Ofgem review is an important opportunity to ensure regulation and our remit keeps pace with change.

“No matter how you arrange the institutions, a strong, independent energy regulator will always be essential. We’re keen to work with industry to cut red tape and back investment, but firm regulation remains vital to protect consumers and maintain confidence in the sector.”

Growth depends on cutting back the energy regulator

By Dhara Vyas, chief executive of Energy UK

Regulators have been in the crosshairs this year. Ofwat is to be scrapped, the chairman of the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) was unceremoniously ousted and strongly worded letters from government have instructed regulators to prioritise growth, clearly recognising that previous regulatory approaches may not be delivering for the economy.

The job of the regulator is not easy – balancing competing priorities while overseeing sectors of crucial importance and facing intense external scrutiny over decisions that can affect every home and business in the country.

Unsurprisingly, every industry has disagreements with their regulator. The energy sector, which we represent, is no exception. Our regulator, Ofgem, should of course hold our industry to high standards and ensure the interests of customers are protected. But they must also hold themselves to the same standards.

With the Government reviewing Ofgem and its future role, there is a growing sense – in our sector and elsewhere – that the energy regulator has lost its way. By becoming too big and bureaucratic, it has overseen a dramatic increase in red tape, reducing growth and pushing up costs for the very customers it tries to protect. And lacking confidence to assert its independence, it is susceptible to external pressure that has fuelled short-term thinking, detracting from effective, efficient regulation.

The Government has committed to reducing the cost of regulation by 25pc by the end of this parliament. Ofgem should be one of the first places it looks as over the last 10 years its headcount has increased by 120pc and its budget by 200pc, while the workforce of the sector it regulates has grown by a mere 8pc. Its market administration costs are over seven times higher than that of the French energy regulator despite a very similar remit.

Ofgem’s mixed performance over recent years does little to justify these numbers.

Poor regulation of the retail market before the energy crisis led to the subsequent collapse of 30 suppliers in 2021-22, costing billpayers around £2.5bn, with the regulator’s inaction heavily criticised by Citizens Advice and the public accounts committee.

Ofgem’s reluctance to allow sector investment ahead of need, particularly for network infrastructure, cost billpayers almost £2bn during the energy crisis alone, according to our calculations. Earlier investment in the network to anticipate future demand – rather than waiting until problems arise from a lack of capacity – would have reduced the constraint costs that come from switching off wind farms and replacing with gas generation.

In each case, Ofgem subsequently made efforts to improve, but not before consumers were left on the hook for billions of pounds of regulatory failures that have cast a long shadow over the economy. The price of underinvestment in network infrastructure is clear: higher constraint costs and network charges that will have to rise very sharply to fund urgently needed development.

Failing to take the long-term view has been accompanied by knee-jerk responses to media or political noise, diverting time and resources – and the industry’s – into unwarranted investigations where the benefit to customers is unclear.

Recent examples include back-billing, which even Ofgem admitted wasn’t a growing or systemic problem, and low standing charge proposals, which pre-empted the regulator’s own review and flew in the face of evidence from industry and consumer groups that moving costs from the standing charge to the unit rate would make energy more expensive for many vulnerable households. Ofgem’s own analysis found this and Citizens Advice also warned as much.

Meanwhile household energy debt, the most serious problem facing customers and the industry, has reached a record £4.5
billion, more than doubling over the last two years.

Combining confused priorities with an ever-growing workforce encourages a bureaucratic and onerous approach – symbolised by industry codes that are over 10,000 pages long. The result is a heavy regulatory burden that does little for customers, driving up costs, and actively diverting the sector from efforts to innovate.

Regulators must be independent enough to stay above the fray and take the right long-term decisions, not the ones that might look good today. Focus unduly on your profile and there is a temptation to be seen to crack down on your sector. Of course, Ofgem should act when there are clear breaches, but when it starts to appear performative, it breeds mistrust.

As we set out in our recommendations today, it’s time for a radical reform of Ofgem’s brief, refocusing on its core role as an economic regulator, balancing the interests of current and future consumers in its approach to infrastructure investment.

Breaking up Ofgem – with its consumer protection and competition role moving to the CMA, and scheme delivery oversight shifting to other suitable organisations – will allow it to concentrate on the right priorities.

When the Government concludes its review, it should radically reduce Ofgem’s duties and set a new strategic direction to drive long-term customer value through enabling investment and cutting administrative burden. Government must then step back and let Ofgem do its job independently and refrain from pressuring it into unnecessary interventions that blow it off course.

Ofgem should take a leaf out of the Financial Conduct Authority’s (FCA) book by reducing the regulatory burden and working with industry not against it. Standards can be maintained without thousands of pages of specific guidance as the FCA is actively demonstrating by cutting swathes from its handbook.

Ofgem should also be encouraged to enhance skills and capabilities by prioritising quality over quantity. The ability to hire outside of civil service salary bands is crucial to attracting the right talent.

Fixing regulation may not be exciting but it is important. Do that and a vital sector for households, businesses and the economy can be healthier, attract the vital investment that our country so sorely needs, and better able to deliver on what its customers need.

data.title: So, two years on, will a foreign state end up wielding power over The Telegraph?
data.description:
We journalists usually do not like writing about our own newspaper in our own newspaper. The readers expect the engine of news, features and comment to drive forward efficiently: why should they be made to look under the bonnet?

There are times, however, when our readers need to know what is happening. These usually relate to ownership. For a free press, the right ownership underpins confidence for staff and readers alike.

Nearly two years ago, the Editor let me use this space to discuss our ownership. The column’s headline read “It would be unforgivable to allow Abu Dhabi to nationalise The Telegraph.”

At that time, IMI, an investment vehicle of the ruling family in Abu Dhabi, was trying to buy the paper, via the complicated manoeuvre of taking on the debt of the previous owners, the Barclay family. IMI’s junior co-bidders were an American company, RedBird.

The Telegraph editorial team opposed this, arguing that foreign state ownership, however well intentioned, was bound to destroy editorial independence and the readers’ trust. A foreign state inevitably puts its own interests above our duty to print the news as we find it.

It turned out there was no British legislation which prevented foreign state ownership of newspapers. Under pressure in the House of Lords, the then Conservative government passed such a law. As a result, the IMI/RedBird sale could not go through. The Telegraph was for sale once again.

The independent directors appointed to see the paper through the debt problem were kept on by the Department for Culture, Media and Sport (DCMS). This was intended as a short-term measure. Yet here we and they are, nearly two years on, and the ownership of The Telegraph is still not settled. That is why I am troubling the readers once more, and why my headline today is not much different from that of November 2023.

After the sale was announced, several potential bidders appeared, but most backed off because of price. RedBird IMI, poorly advised about their likely success, had overpaid. They valued The Telegraph at about £500m and wanted to get that back. They were deeply fearful of losing face if they sold for less, and RedBird was deeply fearful of their displeasure. Most bidders felt that £350-400m was nearer the mark, but RedBird IMI held out for more.

There was a long, long hiatus, damaging to our business. The British Government, keen to court Abu Dhabi for wider investment reasons (a polite way of saying it is desperate for its money), indulged RedBird IMI as they tried various unsuccessful sale schemes. The DCMS hid behind its “quasi-judicial” role, playing for time and saying little. Ten Downing Street hid behind the DCMS.

In May this year, with some éclat, the prospect of a deal at last hit town. It was announced that RedBird Capital, a separate entity from the RedBird IMI partnership (though run by many of the same RedBird people) would now buy The Telegraph.

Gerry Cardinale, RedBird’s Managing Partner, said he was taking personal charge. His company would pay the full price and own the lion’s share. Abu Dhabi would keep 15 per cent. Other investors would come in. The Telegraph’s journalistic values would be secure, and its commercial future would be bright. The British establishment mobilised in favour of the deal.

Some people said the RedBird move was a device by which its partnership with IMI would, for the purposes of The Telegraph, take a different form, while allowing RedBird’s role as a sort of personal shopper for the Arabs to continue. The reality of the deal, they said, was that Abu Dhabi influence over The Telegraph would not go away. This Mr Cardinale and other RedBird people indignantly denied.

As summer moved into autumn, however, the full deal seemed elusive. Len Blavatnik and Lord Rothermere’s Daily Mail group were said to be in for just under 10 per cent each, but other possibles refused. RedBird did not submit its promised deal to the scrutiny of the DCMS.

Telegraph journalists seeking, as is their job, to investigate what was going on, aroused Mr Cardinale’s ire. The senior editorial team became increasingly concerned that RedBird seemed to have no business plan for The Telegraph’s long-term development and had gone quiet about provisions for editorial independence.

There was also a delay in publishing The Telegraph’s accounts. When outside journalists asked questions about this, an angry Mr Cardinale said he would publish a story saying the accounts were all right. Yet how could he without interfering with editorial independence? Indeed, why was he claiming any right to order anything when, by law, the rights of ownership are frozen until the deal wins government approval?

Further journalistic inquiries into RedBird by The Telegraph itself prompted Mr Cardinale to threaten he would go to war with our entire newsroom. There were apparent media briefings that the excellent current Editor, Chris Evans, would be removed and replaced as Editor by Matthew Garrahan of the Financial Times, a vocal critic of Brexit and of this paper’s attitude to migrants via a social media account which he recently expunged.

Naturally after so much delay, Telegraph journalists had hoped that a deal for the paper could reach a safe haven. But RedBird’s behaviour has undermined our confidence in its editorial intentions, the coherence of its plans and the sources of its money.

On October 10, RedBird finally submitted to the DCMS what it says is its Telegraph deal. We still do not know, however, whether it has raised all the money for the excessive price, or whether it will try to get round any shortfall by offering an “earn-out” on future profits to IMI which would give Abu Dhabi continuing power over the paper.

So Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, has to consider several problems in deciding whether to refer the RedBird bid to the Competition and Markets Authority and to Ofcom.

These include: does the bid satisfy competition and media plurality rules (the involvement of the Daily Mail group may be relevant here)? Does the bid, given the continuing, potentially active role of Abu Dhabi, conform with public-interest rules on free expression of opinion and accurate news? The Government itself has said that it has a “low bar” for intervention in the public interest.

And who is paying what? Investment funds like RedBird never disclose who their investors are. In the sale of a national newspaper, such concealment raises a real issue of trust.

This is sharpened by the possible issue of China. RedBird has insisted that no “Chinese state money” is involved in The Telegraph bid and no “Chinese LP [Limited Partners] in RedBird funds” either. But the chairman of RedBird Capital, John Thornton, ex-Goldman Sachs, has held positions in Chinese state institutions and is one of the Chinese regime’s most trusted Western interlocutors. RedBird have not said that no individual Chinese investors are involved. Under Xi Jinping’s totalitarianism, China’s wealthiest cannot escape his tight leash.

In this paper on Wednesday, Iain Duncan-Smith, the former Conservative leader, raised the Chinese dimension, calling for a Foreign State Intervention Notice. It will fall to the Cabinet Office minister, Darren Jones, to decide whether to launch a national security investigation too, as has been suggested by Tory and Liberal Democrat peers.

I hope I have said enough, dear readers, to show that the process of buying this newspaper has been protracted, opaque and disquieting. If the Government mishandles it, the outcome will be unsafe for us and for British journalism.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Thames Water named Britain’s worst supplier after surge in sewage dumps
data.description:
Thames Water has been named Britain’s worst water company after a surge in sewage dumping.

The Environment Agency (EA) on Thursday gave the company its lowest rating out of nine suppliers, based on sewage spills and other incidents polluting water sources.

Thames was the only company to receive a one-star rating this year for its environmental performance, down from two stars last year.

It follows a sharp increase in the number of serious pollution incidents at the troubled supplier. The number of serious incidents more than doubled to 33 during 2024, up from 14 in the previous year.

Together with Southern Water and Yorkshire Water, the three companies were responsible for 81pc of serious incidents in 2024.

Thames Water was also responsible for the most pollution cases overall during the year, accounting for 523 of 2,801 incidents across nine major suppliers.

Problems continue 

In January, Thames was found to have released raw sewage into a chalk stream near St Albans, Hertfordshire, feeding into the River Ver, for more than 1,000 hours.

Feargal Sharkey, the Undertones singer turned environmental campaigner, told The Telegraph: “It’s becoming increasingly more difficult to retain a sense of humour, both with this Government and these regulators.

“Clearly, the Government has no control over this industry. The regulators have no control over it whatsoever.”

He added: “What the Government needs is to do is intervene this afternoon, remove the chairman of Ofwat, remove the chairman of the EA and put Thames Water into special administration. We need to take control of this industry.

“As of right now, it’s simply continuing 35 years of abuse of customers and of the environment.”

The same EA rankings show England’s water companies received the lowest ever score for their environmental performance in 2024. Serious pollution incidents increased by 60pc across all nine water companies.

Their poor performance was attributed to last year’s wet and stormy weather, underinvestment in and poor maintenance of infrastructure and increased monitoring and inspection.

Alan Lovell, the chairman of the EA, said: “This year’s results are poor and must serve as a clear and urgent signal for change.”

A separate report by Ofwat, also published on Thursday, showed pollution incidents “remain at unacceptable levels”, with only two companies reporting a reduction in cases in the five years between 2020 and 2025.

Ofwat also named Thames Water as one of its poorest overall performers during the five-year period, adding that it needed to make material improvements across multiple areas including leakage and internal sewer flooding.

Giles Bristow, the head of the campaign group Surfers Against Sewage, said: “Water companies are under more scrutiny than ever, yet their standards keep slipping. Imagine what they’ll try to get away with once the spotlight fades.”

Thames Water has been mired in crisis as its performance deteriorates and its debts rack up. The company’s creditors have been locked in talks with Ofwat to hammer out a rescue plan that would avoid nationalisation.

This would involve writing off a considerable chunk of its near-£20bn debt pile and more leniency on sewage fines.

A spokesman for Thames Water said: “We know we need to further improve for our customers, communities and the environment, and that is why we have embarked on the largest ever investment programme, delivering the biggest upgrade to our network in 150 years.

“Transforming Thames is a major programme of work that will take time; it will take at least a decade to achieve the scale of change required.”

Emma Reynolds, the Environment Secretary, has set out plans to toughen up on penalties for water companies. 

The department announced on Tuesday that it is consulting on proposals that would make it simpler for the EA to fine companies up to £500,000.

data.title: Thames Water lenders prepared to take £7bn hit to avoid nationalisation
data.description:
A group of Thames Water creditors is prepared to effectively write off £7.5bn of debt in a bid to avoid nationalisation.

The consortium has proposed a turnaround plan that would cancel around a third of Thames Water’s near-£20bn debt pile and commit the group to investing in the company.

Outstanding fines would be paid – but the investor group, dubbed London & Valley Water, would also seek leniency on future penalties in exchange for investment.

Thames Water revealed over the summer that annual losses had hit £1.6bn as a soaring debt burden and continued sewage failures fuelled its financial crisis.

Bosses said it would take at least a decade to turn around Britain’s largest water company.

The company’s creditors are looking to secure backing for their plans to avoid the utility being put into a temporary special administration regime, which would effectively wipe out their investments.

Lenders said they would not pay a dividend while conducting their turnaround plans or until the company is listed.

They also committed not to sell the business before the end of March 2030.

The consortium said the “more ambitious” plan would increase investment in Thames Water by around £1bn compared to proposals they submitted to Ofwat in May.

The latest proposal was the result of “three months of detailed discussion” with the water regulator and would “support long-term financial resilience and is intended to help the company pay for outstanding fines”, it added.

Mike McTighe, a City veteran whose current roles include chairman of Telegraph Media Group, would become chairman of Thames Water under the creditors’ proposals.

He said: “There is a huge amount of work to be done to turn around Thames Water and deliver the improved service and environmental outcomes that customers and local communities deserve.

“From day one, we will inject billions in new investment, strengthen Thames Water’s balance sheet, transform the company for thousands of hard-working frontline staff and begin the delivery of an operational turnaround that puts 16 million customers and the environment first.”

Lenders of Thames Water’s “Class A” debt would receive at least 10pc of new equity in the utility company in exchange for writing off about 25pc of their stake, worth around £4bn.

Thames Water said it remains in discussions over the bid but any agreement would be subject to High Court approval.

Chris Weston, the chief executive of Thames Water, said: “Today is an important milestone in our ongoing work with creditors and stakeholders to secure a market-led recapitalisation that establishes the financial and regulatory foundations required to support the investment and performance improvements our customers expect.”

data.title: Virgin Atlantic names new chief as it battles Heathrow over third runway costs
data.description:
Virgin Atlantic has announced the departure of its long-serving chief executive Shai Weiss and named a new boss to lead its high-stakes battle with Heathrow over the bill for a third runway.

Dutchman Corneel Koster will assume the leadership on January 1 as Mr Weiss, who has led Sir Richard Branson’s airline for seven years, stands down. Mr Koster, 54, is currently Virgin’s chief customer and operating officer and has worked at the company in two stints spanning nine years.

He will take charge during a crucial period of negotiations with Heathrow, Virgin’s London base. Ministers are likely to have chosen a design for a new runway by the time Mr Koster becomes chief executive. That will put fresh pressure on airlines including Virgin, which fear the plan will mean a jump in landing charges to pay for the work.

Heathrow’s preferred expansion plan, which includes moving the M25 motorway, comes with a £49bn price tag, including new terminals.

When the plan was detailed in August, Virgin said it supported growth but criticised the current charging mechanism for Heathrow, administered by the Civil Aviation Authority. It said the runway would be “ultimately paid for by consumers and airlines through higher charges.”

Virgin has argued that other major airports have delivered capacity improvements at a far lower cost, with Mr Weiss arguing that Heathrow should be broken up into competing terminals in order to bring down prices.

The CAA said in July it would review the suitability of the current charging model for funding Heathrow’s expansion and consider alternatives.

Mr Koster will also have to decide whether to re-establish a base for Virgin at Gatwick, which last month secured Labour backing to go ahead with plans to turn an emergency landing strip into a fully fledged runway, greatly increasing its capacity.

Virgin pulled out of Gatwick during Covid and never returned. Mr Weiss has been sceptical about the business case for doing so, though Sir Richard has made more positive noises.

Mr Weiss’s exit will coincide with the end of Virgin’s five-year strategy cycle. The airline said that he and Mr Koster had been working for months on a new plan, which will “not be a massive move away from what we have been doing”.

Part of that agenda was revealed in July when Virgin announced initiatives including the rollout of free Wi-Fi provided by Elon Musk’s Starlink, a completely new app and an AI-powered virtual concierge service that the airline says will outdo Amazon’s Alexa.

Mr Koster’s stints at Virgin have been separated by a period at minority shareholder Delta Air Lines, which jointly approved his appointment with Sir Richard.

The new chief’s contacts at Delta and reputation as a safe pair of hands at carriers that have also included KLM are understood to have been a factor in his selection.

Virgin insiders said he also grasped the importance of the quirkier aspects of the airline’s culture. It seeks to project itself as a rebel in contrast to the more buttoned up British Airways.

One close colleague said: “Corneel is a real mix of hardcore commercial and really strong operational experience. But he’s also got a lot of heart about him and gets that Virgin take on things, the slightly different style we have, and the warmth and all that stuff. He’s a great ambassador for that.”

The Dutchman demonstrated his willingness while standing in for Mr Weiss during a trip to Austin in 2022, leading cabin crew and local dignitaries in a country dance routine.

The colleague said: “He dressed up as a cowboy to lead the line-dancing. You knew then that he fully understood the DNA of the company. He’s got a great sense of humour and can be quite cheeky.”

Mr Weiss, 57, revealed he had been diagnosed with stage three colon cancer in 2022. Virgin said that he initially planned to spend time with his family and travel, having been given the all-clear.

A spokesman said: “He’s not going to a new role. He’s going to genuinely take some very well deserved time off and do a bit of travelling. He wants to go to Japan, which he loves.”

Mr Weiss, a British citizen who was born and raised in Israel, joined Virgin Atlantic as chief financial officer in 2014 before serving as commercial chief and becoming chief executive in 2019. He helped the airline survive a brush with bankruptcy during Covid and earlier this year the business posted its first profit since 2016.

data.title: Higher water bills are a shameless reward for failure
data.description:
On the face of it, the news that water suppliers won’t be allowed to ramp up bills by as much as they were hoping is cause for celebration.

At first glance, it looks like an all-too-rare rebuff for an industry that has been thumbing its nose at customers for far too long – which is, of course, precisely how this latest sorry charade is meant to be interpreted.

Ministers are desperate for us all to think that after decades of wrongdoing, it has taken a Labour government to finally get tough with the cowboys that pollute our rivers and charge us a fortune for the privilege, all while absentee shareholders and negligent executives line their own pockets with money that could’ve been spent on investment.

Yet, that isn’t what is happening here. Not even close.

Yes, bills won’t be increasing to the extent that suppliers were asking for – but the point is that an independent panel of “experts” has still sanctioned further rises beyond what Ofwat had approved and most objective observers thought those rises were already pretty steep.

So the idea that this is somehow a big victory for households and a defeat for water companies is profound nonsense. The opposite is true.

In fact, it is impossible to see further bill hikes as anything other than a reward for failure for an industry that has long treated customers and the environment with complete contempt.

Not that Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, seems even remotely prepared to acknowledge what is really happening here.

“We are laser-focused on helping ease the cost-of-living pressure on households. We’ve frozen fuel duty, raised the minimum wage and pensions and brought down mortgage rates, putting more money in people’s pockets,” she said – though hopefully not with a straight face.

This is gaslighting territory. It takes a special kind of shamelessness to boast about making people better off at the precise moment that millions of families are having a fresh round of bill rises imposed on them.

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) said on Thursday it is allowing five water companies – Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, Southern Water, South East Water and Wessex Water – to lift bills by up to 5pc over the next five years.

That might seem fairly tame if it wasn’t for the fact that it comes on top of an average increase of 24pc that industry regulator Ofwat had already approved in April.

As Mike Keil, chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), pointed out: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.”

In fact, two in five of the households these companies serve say they will struggle to afford the increases coming down their pipe, according to the CCW, which represents water customers across England and Wales.

What’s more, the CCW has conducted its own analysis showing there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs to cut bills by around £41 a year – “but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return,” Keil said.

However, you would’ve thought otherwise when reading Hardy’s hollow words on Thursday.

“I understand the public anger over bill rises – that’s why I expect every water company to offer proper support to anyone struggling to pay,” she said.

But as Keil said: “Three of these five companies also have no plans to end water poverty by 2030.”

The CMA doesn’t come out of this much better. It makes a big song and dance about how the additional funding will ensure “essential improvements”, such as more resilient supply and a reduction in pollution, are delivered “at reasonable cost”.

Most people will accept some level of inflation in their household expenses but I’m willing to bet that few regard a 27pc surge as reasonable – even if it is over five years.

Some customers are already grappling with rises close to, or more than, 50pc once inflation is added because suppliers have been outrageously front-loading increases to help pay for long-overdue infrastructure improvements.

The reality is that bill-payers are being asked to subsidise an era of playing catch-up because water suppliers failed in the past to keep pace with investment, maintenance and environmental duties.

The money was there but instead of being spent modernising the system, repairing antiquated pipes and ensuring sewage wasn’t pouring into our waterways, it was being lavished on greedy investors and weak-kneed bosses or vanishing straight out the door in the form of heavy debt repayments.

Research from the University of Greenwich has found that 35p in every pound spent on customer bills is swallowed up in debt and dividend costs.

With the system buckling, it is surely no coincidence that these are the highest bill increases since privatisation.

It is also no surprise that the suppliers that went crying to the CMA are also among the most debt-laden in a sector that has long been leveraged up to the gills.

Meanwhile, the competition policeman quietly concedes that alongside a much-trumpeted round of additional investment, bill increases will also help pay down debt and boost returns for investors – many of whom are the same faceless funds that presided over the current wretched state of affairs in the first place.

For all the talk of long overdue reform and a new era of genuine accountability from Labour, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that our broken water industry has effectively been handed a huge taxpayer-funded bailout.

data.title: Climate disaster science gets huge coverage. When it’s found to be wrong... crickets
data.description:
In the world of climate politics, highly pessimistic scenarios can drive headlines and coverage, and so in turn have an impact on policy. Two recent unfortunate episodes show that the rush to shout “fire” means that some scenarios are gaining massive influence when they should instead be attracting sceptical reviews of the science used to construct them.

Take wildfires. Despite repeated claims of a “world on fire”, data set after data set shows that the world burns ever less in terms of burned area. Disrupting this unhelpful reality, a splashy paper in Nature last year finally found a worrying narrative: “extreme wildfire events” had more than doubled globally over the past two decades. Using satellite data from 2003 to 2023, the authors clustered fire hotspots and tallied their “fire radiative power” – a proxy for intensity. Predictably, this ignited a media bonfire. Outlets from The New York Times to CBS News blared warnings of a planet ablaze, seeing fiercer fires as proof that we are hurtling toward a global inferno.

Hold the extinguishers. New research indicates that the study was wrong. The new analysis shows that extreme fire is down by 35 per cent over the same period. The fires are burning less intensely, and we already knew that less area is getting burned as time goes by – but this contradicts the extreme climate script, so we heard crickets from major media outlets.

There’s more good news we are not hearing much about: 2025 is shaping up as one of the least fiery years on record across most areas of the world. Total burned area in Africa, the Americas, Asia, and Europe is dramatically down compared to previous years – potentially the lowest in the 21st century if the trend holds. We should celebrate this as evidence that adaptation and better land management are working. Instead, such news is buried because it doesn’t align with the orthodoxy of economy-crushing climate policies.

The biggest advert for those policies was last year’s bombshell study – also in Nature – claiming that global real income will be 19 percent lower by 2050 than it would have been with no climate change and 62 per cent lower by 2100. Alarmingly, the research became the go-to damage estimate for the Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS), a powerhouse coalition of central banks and regulators spanning the US, EU, Japan, and beyond.

For decades, solid economics – from Nobel laureate climate economist William Nordhaus and others – has painted a clear picture: climate change is a serious but manageable economic issue, costing 2-3 per cent of global GDP by century’s end. That is the same as enduring one or two recessions over 75 years – hardly societal collapse. But the new research seemed to indicate that the economic damage caused by climate change would be far worse than had been thought.

The UK Office for Budget Responsibility and others cited it in important ways because it aligned with their bias toward the narrative of apocalypse.

Now, several peer-reviewed takedowns – again published in Nature itself – are ripping the new science to shreds, as even the Wall Street Journal has acknowledged. Errors abound: most of the suggested economic damage disappears once data from Uzbekistan is removed from the calculations. The estimated climate impact, correctly analysed, is statistically indistinguishable from zero.

But the damage is already done. The NGFS, which was co-founded by former Bank of England governor and now Canadian prime minister Mark Carney, has the important role of stress-testing financial systems, and it has been using these unfounded disaster costs to regulate banks, potentially triggering unnecessary regulations that could stifle growth.

As climate researcher Roger Pielke Jr. notes, the NGFS has a history of cherry-picking outlier damages over sober estimates – suggesting that the NGFS needs a root-and-branch overhaul of its approach to climate science. Scientific publishing itself needs to take a look in the mirror.

The key takeaway from the two episodes is that it’s important to turn to comprehensive data – and that goes for the media, too.

Climate change needs a response. The true climate challenge is to develop solutions that do not cause more economic damage than climate change would. Adaptation and green innovation can deliver such solutions. Unfortunately, today’s popular climate policies would instead squander 5-20 per cent of GDP while only cutting emissions marginally.  

We need to embrace data-driven solutions that address climate challenges without torching the global economy.

Bjorn Lomborg is President of the Copenhagen Consensus, Visiting Fellow at Stanford University’s Hoover Institution, and author of “False Alarm” and “Best Things First”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Thames Water’s rescue plan risks setting a dangerous precedent
data.description:
The latest version of a creditor-led rescue plan for Thames Water ticks multiple boxes. One can see why the company, ministers and the industry regulator might be minded to give it the green light in the coming weeks.

The pressure to hammer out a deal is intense. The threat of a multibillion-pound bill landing on Rachel Reeves’s desk before her Budget in November is concentrating Whitehall minds.

Thames is burning cash rapidly. A separate announcement from the company revealed that in August it called on the remaining £628m of a £1.5bn emergency loan.

It is further worrying evidence of the extent to which an organisation providing critical services to 13 million people continues to live hand-to-mouth after years of reckless ownership.

With the clock ticking, the “London and Valley” consortium of lenders has come up with a decent upgrade on the somewhat vague proposal published a month ago.

Then, the prospective new owners declared their undying commitment to “a new transparent and collaborative relationship with regulators”, before outlining a plan lacking in details.

The highlights of their latest attempt include a more generous portion of equity and a bigger debt write-off than previously envisaged. A commitment to not pay dividends for five years, along with the ambition of eventually returning Thames to the stock market are also welcome measures.

Yet all the hard work that has gone into answering many of the concerns of regulators, Government and long-suffering customers risks being overshadowed by what amounts to a fresh attempt to wriggle out of fines.

Having sensibly given up on trying to dodge outstanding penalties for pumping sewage into waterways, Thames’s wannabe owners are now trying to inoculate themselves against future punishment.

“The current regulatory framework for the water industry does not allow it to successfully turnaround its business,” London and Valley says.

The consortium won’t take “no” for an answer, it seems.

Steve Reed, the former environment secretary, told parliament in June that Thames must meet its statutory obligations, after being asked about possible “regulatory easements”.

But the very idea that an industry that treats Britain’s rivers as its dumping ground should face softer environmental targets – or “stretching but achievable incentives” in the consortium’s words – won’t wash.

It’s hard to square this with the consortium’s warm words.

Mike McTighe, the proposed future chairman of Thames – he is also chairman of the company behind The Telegraph – talks about “rebuilding public trust”.

The creditors themselves admit that Thames has presided over “a cycle of underinvestment and underperformance that has driven trust in Thames Water to an all-time low.”

Few would argue with that assessment. Yet any agreement that makes it easier for the company to pollute with greater impunity is an improbable basis for the restoration of Thames’s relationship with the public.

Consider that in an industry that has vandalised the environment, this is a chronic offender.

Thames’s record is getting worse, not better. Some 50pc more raw sewage was discharged into Britain’s waterways from Thames’s ageing system last year, compared with the previous 12 months.

In May, Thames Water was fined £123m by Ofwat – the biggest ever fine imposed by the regulator.

From a purely financial perspective, the creditors’ plea for clemency is understandable.

They expect future fines of more than £1bn if current regulations are applied and argue that something has to give if Thames is to receive the investment it needs.

This financial hole was of Thames’s own making, however. Its creditors bankrolled it as it hollowed itself out. To turn around now and demand that the rules are changed to make pumping effluent into rivers a less costly business is an affront to justice and risks setting a horrible precedent.

Perhaps even now, after months and months of negotiations, the hedge funds that swooped in to buy up debt on the cheap when Thames was on the brink, still don’t understand the extent of public and political anger.

Customer ire stretches to the whole gamut of misdeeds – rising bills, poor service, undeserved dividends and excessive executive pay.

But it is the epidemic of pollution that has attracted the most fury. That the creditors have chosen to attack on that front seems “courageous”, as they say in the Civil Service.

Indeed, I am willing to bet that the majority of people want to see stiffer fines, not softer ones, particularly if the current set-up has failed to act as a deterrent. Weaker rules would surely lead to more pollution, not less.

Meanwhile, the knock-on effect of granting Thames an easier ride would be that every other water company in Britain would be entitled to expect the same exemptions on the basis of fairness.

A reminder from the creditors that their offer is the only credible one left after private equity firm KKR abandoned its bid looks designed to focus minds. Ditto talks of how the plan “will avoid special administration”, and ensure that “Thames Water’s turnaround will not fall to the UK taxpayer”.

Decency, however, demands that Ofwat and the Government don’t allow themselves to be pushed around by a bunch of opportunistic hedge funds.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: JCB to spend £100m on new UK factory
data.description:
JCB has delivered a rare boost for British manufacturing after unveiling plans to spend £100m modernising its flagship UK factory and global headquarters. 

The firm, known for its distinctive yellow diggers and agricultural equipment, will spend the funds radically overhauling its current plant in Rocester, Staffordshire, which has been in use since 1950.

It said that the complex, which was originally converted from a cheese factory 75 years ago, had become an obstacle to boosting production of its machinery equipment. The move will help safeguard 8,000 jobs. 

The modernisation plan comes as the business, controlled by the billionaire Bamford family led by Lord Anthony Bamford, struggles with soaring costs, weak demand and the impact of US steel tariffs.

JCB is one of Britain’s largest privately-owned manufacturing groups, employing 19,000 at 22 construction plants around the world. 

It was founded by Lord Bamford’s father, Joseph Cyril Bamford, in 1945 and today turns over nearly £6bn a year.

While a weaker global market last year caused the business to axe 230 agency workers, it is understood that the investment aims to help current staff become more efficient at building its equipment at speed.

As part of the plans, JCB will construct a fully automated powder-paint facility within the plant at a cost of £60m while changing the layout of the shop floor and upgrading production lines.

Graeme Macdonald, JCB’s chief executive, said the end result will be “unrecognisable”.

“With all the investments we’ve made over the last 20 years in India, the US, China and Brazil, we needed to modernise our headquarters,” he said.

Mr Macdonald added: “You would never design a factory the way it’s laid out today. So we’re taking the opportunity to make it leaner, more efficient and more cost competitive.

“We just couldn’t build enough. We will definitely solve that problem and we can then fulfil demand from all the other parts of the world.”

JCB’s factory overhaul marks a rare vote of confidence in the future for a manufacturing sector that has been in headlong retreat.

UK factories last year consumed the least energy in 50 years, with the decline almost entirely because of shutdowns and closures.

Britain also ranks outside the top 10 manufacturing nations for the first time since the Industrial Revolution, behind countries including Mexico.

The overhaul will be made easier by the opening of a new $500m (£373m) factory in Texas which will begin production next year.

When that plant is up and running, it will be able to take pressure off the Rocester site, which currently fulfils the bulk of US orders.

Mr Macdonald said that once the two projects have been completed, JCB’s annual production capacity will surpass 200,000 machines, compared to the 120,000 built last year.

While the increase in US output will help soften the blow from the extension of Donald Trump’s steel tariffs to cover construction machinery, he said it will still have to absorb hundreds of millions of pounds in additional annual costs.

He predicted that the slump in demand will not ease until 2026 or 2027 as construction firms that made record purchases of machinery in the wake of Covid rein in spending.

Lord Bamford said the UK investment will put the Rocester site “at the forefront of our industry”.

“Obviously, we are expanding overseas, not least in America, where we have been for decades. But the UK is our home,” he said.

data.title: Eurostar orders UK’s first fleet of double-decker trains
data.description:
A major fleet of double-decker trains is to operate in Britain for the first time, but passengers have been warned not to expect twice as many seats.

Eurostar said it will purchase up to 50 two-storey trains from Alstom for use on routes including London St Pancras International to Paris, Brussels and Amsterdam via the Channel Tunnel.

The fully electric fleet will be named Eurostar Celestia, which is derived from the Latin word “caelestis”, meaning “heavenly”.

The order will cost Eurostar €2bn (£1.7bn), yet the trains will have only 20pc more seats than the firm’s existing fleet of 17 single-decker e320s from Siemens.

That is because the carriages will be fitted with interior steps either in the middle or at each end, taking a significant chunk out of the additional space.

They will also be barely six inches taller than the current Siemens trains, with the floor lowered to accommodate the extra deck, requiring people to take a step down when boarding.

Virgin Trains, which plans to launch Channel Tunnel services in competition with Eurostar, said this month that it had decided against ordering the same Avelia Horizon model from Alstom because the stairs would have impacted service standards.

Virgin Trains said that the stairs would hamper the ability to serve at-seat meals, which are part of its efforts to offer a more upmarket service than available on Eurostar.

A limited trial of two double-decker SR Class 4DD trains was conducted on services between Dartford in Kent and Charing Cross in central London in the 1950s and 1960s, but they were withdrawn in 1971 because they were considered too cramped and expensive to maintain.

Gwendoline Cazenave, Eurostar’s chief executive, said the trains will nevertheless offer “exceptional comfort” and “new surprises” for passengers.

Eurostar said it is scheduled to begin receiving the first six Celestia models in January 2031, with commercial services launching in May 2031.

The trains, which will run in pairs through the Channel Tunnel – providing around 1,000 seats – will be the first major fleet of double-deckers on the UK’s railways.

Double-decker trains are a common sight on the Continent, but the vast majority of the UK’s rail network is unable to accommodate them because of problems such as bridges not being high enough.

But the high-speed line between St Pancras and the Channel Tunnel was built to European standards, enabling it to be used by higher trains.

Ms Cazenave said the order is key to Eurostar’s “ambitious growth strategy” of reaching 30 million passengers per year, up from 19.5 million in 2024.

Passengers in all classes of travel will be able to choose between a seat on the upper or lower deck, with no price difference. They will enjoy enhancements such as more legroom and additional areas for bikes and wheelchair users.

Eurostar confirmed an initial order for 30 trains with an option for a further 20. They will operate in addition to the e320s, bringing the total fleet up to 67 trains and enabling more frequent services on existing routes and the addition of new destinations, including Frankfurt and Geneva.

Richard Bowker, a head of National Express and the Strategic Rail Authority, said that while double-decker trains will add some capacity they will also mean longer boarding times at stations, potentially extending journeys.

He said that could be bad news for campaigners seeking the restoration of Eurostar services to Ashford and Ebbsfleet in Kent.

Growing competition

It comes just a week before the Office of Rail and Road (ORR), the industry regulator, is due to choose between three rival attempts to provide competition to Eurostar for the first time in its 30-year history.

Sir Richard Branson’s Virgin has agreed a deal for 12 Avelia Stream trains from Alstom, the successor to the Pendolino tilting trains it once operated in the UK.

Italy’s state-owned FS Italiane Group plans to operate the same Frecciarossa 1000 trains it runs between Milan and Paris. Meanwhile, Gemini Trains, chaired by Labour peer Lord Berkeley, would purchase single-decker trains from Siemens.

Eurostar will be forced to spend €80m to expand the only UK maintenance facility for Channel Tunnel trains. The remaining space at the Temple Mills site in London is to be handed over to the new entrant chosen by the ORR.

Eurostar is majority-owned by the French state railway company SNCF Connect.

The UK sold its stake in the operator to private companies for £757m in 2015.

data.title: The £5 chocolate bar that shows why food prices are going through the roof
data.description:
If you want a snapshot of how far food prices have climbed over the past two years, just head into your local newsagent and pick up a bar of Dairy Milk.

Taking home that distinctive purple wrapper will now set you back around £5 for a large 360g bar, 25pc more than in September 2023.

Cocoa prices have more than doubled over the past couple of years as a result of poor harvests in West Africa, including Ghana and the Ivory Coast. Manufacturers have pushed up prices, made chocolate bars smaller and changed recipes to cope with rising costs.

Yet the weather is only partly to blame. Rachel Reeves’s tax raid, Brexit and even a net zero packaging tax are all driving prices higher – and are expected to do so for months to come.

Official data published on Wednesday showed the headline rate of inflation stood at 3.8pc in September, defying expectations of a rise to 4pc.

Monthly food and drink prices actually fell by 0.2pc in September compared with August. This took the annual rate of supermarket inflation down from 5.1pc to 4.5pc, according to the Office for National Statistics (ONS).

Grant Fitzner, chief economist at the ONS, said the monthly fall offered “some glimmer of hope”.

But these headline figures mask double-digit increases in the cost of meat, butter, coffee and chocolate, rises that the average Briton will be feeling keenly.

After hovering under £2,000 per tonne for half a decade, cocoa prices have shot up over the past couple of years to reach £4,500 today, although they are a far cry from the record high of £9,100 at the start of the year.

The surge has forced many chocolate makers to push up prices or even change the recipes of their products. Biscuit giant McVitie’s has reduced the cocoa content in its Penguin and Club bars so much that the snacks can no longer be classed as chocolate.

A slogan on Club bars that used to read, “If you like a lot of chocolate on your biscuit, join our Club”, now says, “If you like a lot of biscuit in your break, join our Club.”

It’s not just chocolate. Coffee prices have risen sharply over the past year amid prolonged droughts in key producing regions, including Brazil. Beef, milk and butter prices are also up by 26.5pc, 12pc and 17.5pc respectively compared to a year ago. (Milk is a key ingredient in chocolate too.)

Farmers say poor grass growth is primarily to blame, following England’s second–worst harvest on record. A lack of government support post-Brexit has also been blamed for the fact that 5pc fat beef mince now easily costs more than £5 a packet.

Analysis by the Energy & Climate Intelligence Unit (ECIU) earlier this week showed that the five items listed above account for nearly two fifths of all recent food price inflation, despite accounting for just over £1 in every £10 of the average weekly shop.

Anna Taylor at the Food Foundation says “extreme weather” is to blame. “This is a worrying trend. [The] Government must treat this as a food security issue, and take steps to build the resilience of farming and our supply chains to shocks.”

Weather is just one piece of the puzzle, however. The Chancellor must also share part of the blame for fuelling a rise in the cost of the weekly shop.

The British Retail Consortium (BRC) has warned that food prices are likely to keep on rising for the rest of the year as bosses struggle to absorb £7bn in extra costs from higher employer National Insurance, an increased minimum wage and a new net zero packaging tax.

Extended producer responsibility (EPR) was intended to simplify how we pay for recycling. The idea is relatively simple: the people who make packaging should foot the bill instead of households via their council tax bills.

However, placing the burden on manufacturers will add 12p to the cost of a bottle of wine, 6p to a bottle of beer and 18p per bottle of spirits, according to the Food and Drink Federation. The Bank of England has warned the policy could push up food inflation by 0.5pc.

The BRC estimates it will add £2bn of costs across the sector, in addition to an estimated bill of £2.33bn for National Insurance increases.

Supermarket bosses worry more tax rises could be on the way. Reeves is seeking to raise around £30bn in next month’s Budget, and many businesses fear they could be targeted.

The boss of Lidl warned against a fresh raid on Wednesday. Ryan McDonnell, the head of the German discounter’s UK division, said: “We’ve been very clear that putting more pressure on business will only fuel inflationary pressure.”

September’s unchanged headline inflation figure was a welcome surprise and economists are beginning to hope the UK may have turned a corner.

However, the rate at which food and drink prices are climbing still remains far too fast. September’s 4.5pc rate compares with an average of only 0.1pc from 2015-2019.

The Chancellor is keenly aware of this. “I am not satisfied with these numbers,” Reeves said in response to the latest ONS figures.

Inflation can prove to be political poison, with rapid price rises leaving voters dissatisfied. The Chancellor said she was “determined to ensure we support people struggling with higher bills and the cost of living challenges”. Reeves is considering increasing taxes on higher-earning families to bankroll a cut in poorer households’ energy bills, The Telegraph reported last week.

Yet that will do little to bring down the price of a Dairy Milk. If food inflation continues at its current rate, a £5 bar of chocolate will only leave voters feeling bitter despite the sweet taste.
