data.title: British banker handed $25m by Wall Street giant
data.description:
A British banker has been handed a $25m (£18.7m) bonus after being promoted to chairman of Wall Street giant Citi.

Jane Fraser, who has served as Citi’s chief executive since 2021, has been elected to chair the board, cementing her position at the top of the bank.

She replaces John Dugan, who has been in the role since 2019 and will now become lead independent director. Citi has kept its chairman and chief executive roles separate since 2007. The $25m payout consists of restricted stock and more than one million stock options.

Ms Fraser, who was born in the seaside golfing town of St Andrews, is the most senior British banker in the US.

The Cambridge and Harvard-educated executive spent more than a decade at consultancy giant McKinsey before joining Citi in 2004 and rising through a number of senior roles.

The 58-year-old, who speaks fluent Spanish, fended off sexist backlash in the Mexican press after being put in charge of the bank’s Latin American operations.

She became chief executive in 2021, just over a year after her predecessor, Michael Corbat, said he did not expect to be succeeded by a woman.

‘She has the vision’

During her tenure, Ms Fraser has overseen the biggest shake-up of the Wall Street bank for more than two decades, reorganising the business to strip back layers of management and hand herself more control. Citi last year said it would cut 20,000 jobs – around 10pc of its global workforce – as part of the restructuring.

Citi, one of the five largest banks in the US, has lagged behind its rivals and come under pressure from investors to improve its profitability.

But there have been signs of improvement in recent years. The bank reported a 16pc jump in profit to $3.75bn in the third quarter, while its share price has more than doubled over the last five years.

Mr Dugan said: “Jane’s very deliberate plan to make Citi a simpler and more focused bank has created meaningful shareholder value.

“She has the vision, drive and determination to ensure that Citi not only builds on this progress but thrives in this era of both continued macro uncertainty and unprecedented innovation,” he added.

Ms Fraser said: “As we get more and more of the hard yards behind us, my excitement about what’s possible for Citi grows exponentially.

“We’re standing shoulder to shoulder with clients navigating pressures and opportunities coming at them from every direction, we have a world-class team that’s committed to strengthening every aspect of our performance, and we’ve shown that we can meaningfully grow our returns for our shareholders.”

data.title: London buses ‘infested with cockroaches’
data.description:
London buses are infested with cockroaches, drivers have claimed.

Cockroaches have been spotted aboard dozens of Transport for London (TfL) buses and in drivers’ rest areas, prompting safety campaigners to described the situation as “untenable”.

Transport bosses have launched an investigation after London bus safety campaigners raised the issue in a letter, more than a year after The Telegraph first revealed the scale of the problem.

Passengers have also noticed the infestation. This week, Matilda O’Flaherty, a 21-year-old student, told The Telegraph she had seen two cockroaches during a journey on a number 17 bus in August.

Describing how one had emerged from a hole in the seat before crawling up her leg, she said: “It was so gross, and it was about the size of my thumb.”

One driver on route 159 told the London Bus Forums group he had found a cockroach in his drink. In a video recorded inside the vehicle, he said: “I went to take a sip from my drink and felt something in my mouth. I spat it out and saw it was a cockroach. I felt sick and ended up vomiting.”

Elsewhere, the group named buses on routes 159, 345 and 55 as suffering infestations.

Kevin Mustafa, a London bus safety campaigner, said it was more than an “occasional nuisance”, with “many drivers describing infestations”.

He told the BBC on Monday: “The current state is untenable for drivers who already face demanding working conditions. London bus drivers are reporting persistent issues with cockroaches and unsanitary conditions onboard their vehicles.

“This is not a matter of occasional nuisance. Many drivers describe infestations, and the situation is starting to pose real health, safety and well-being risks.”

Cockroaches are typically associated with unhygienic environments, although earlier this year the insects were also reported to be plaguing NHS hospitals earlier this year.

Last year, Mr Mustafa, claimed TfL’s contracted bus operators were “not cleaning the buses properly” and that “at least once a week” he heard a report of insects aboard a vehicle.

A TfL spokesman said instances of cockroaches being reported aboard buses were “rare”, adding: “We are committed to working together with operators to provide a clean environment for staff and customers.

“All buses are also cleaned every night before entering service and reports such as these are rare. We take any report seriously, and are urgently investigating the specific incidents that have been raised.

“As with any reports, we will take appropriate action as part of the investigation, which can include taking buses out of service for inspection, deep cleans and treatment with specialist equipment if necessary.

“We would like to reassure staff that our operators would never take action against people raising concerns about welfare or safety and encourage any driver with concerns to contact their employer, their union, or contact us directly.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Millions of households to face higher water bills
data.description:
Millions of households are facing higher-than-expected water bills after the competition watchdog allowed five utility companies to increase their charges.

Following a provisional ruling by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, South East Water, Southern Water and Wessex Water will be able to increase bills by a collective £556m over the next five years.

While that is less than the £2.7bn requested by the companies, it will still lead to some households paying annual bills of up to £638 a year.

Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, said she understood the public’s anger over bill rises, but expected companies to offer support to those struggling to pay.

Between them, Anglian and Northumbrian have been allowed to raise bills by a further 1pc, while Southern, South East and Wessex have been handed increases of 3pc, 4pc and 5pc, respectively.

Together, they serve around seven million customers across the UK.

The ruling means that Anglian will charge an average bill of £599, while Northumbrian bill payers will pay £495. South East Water will increase bills to £322, while Southern will charge £638.

Wessex was handed the biggest increase of 5pc, meaning its bills will rise to £622. All had asked for larger increases.

Appeal to the CMA

It comes after the companies appealed to the CMA earlier this year, requesting permission to increase their bills higher than Ofwat, the water regulator, had previously ruled.

In defence of its decision, the CMA said the extra money would help reduce pollution and strengthen supply, although it also said it would help cover the companies’ rising debt costs.

Kirstin Baker, who chaired an independent group of experts appointed by the CMA, said: “We’ve found that water companies’ requests for significant bill increases, on top of those allowed by Ofwat, are largely unjustified.

“We understand the real pressure on household budgets and have worked to keep increases to a minimum, while still ensuring there is funding to deliver essential improvements at a reasonable cost.”

Thames Water, Britain’s biggest water company, had also appealed, but its proceedings have been put on hold while it battles to stave off nationalisation.

Ms Hardy said efforts were being made to restore the public’s trust in water companies.

She said: “We’ve made sure that investment cash goes into infrastructure upgrades, not bonuses, and we’re creating a tough new regulator to clean up our waterways and restore trust in the system.”

It comes amid growing anger over the state of the water sector, which has been responsible for a rise in sewage failures in recent years.

Mike Keil, the chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), said: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.

“CCW’s own analysis suggests there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs, cutting bills by around £41 a year, but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return.

“There is a danger the customers of these companies will end up paying more, without seeing any additional improvements in return.”

Joanna Elson, the chief executive of Independent Age, a charity, said higher bills would be “extremely damaging to people on low incomes living in these areas”.

She said: “The people in later life we support tell us the drastic lengths they are taking to reduce their water costs, including only washing a couple of times a week, not flushing the toilet and cutting down on laundry. This shouldn’t be happening in modern Britain.”

Ms Elson added: “We urge the UK Government to protect customers on low incomes from rising water bills by establishing a national water social tariff. This will end the postcode lottery.

 “It’s not right that your address dictates how much financial support you receive.”

data.title: Peter Hitchens criticises New Statesman for using late brother’s image on gossip column
data.description:
When the Left-wing New Statesman magazine decided to name a new diary column after the late Christopher Hitchens, it was meant to be a tribute to a much-loved colleague.

Instead, the magazine – which chose to illustrate the column with a picture of Hitchens smoking – has been accused by his brother of insensitivity and poor taste because he died of oesophageal cancer.

Peter Hitchens, whose Right-wing views are at odds with the magazine’s editorial stance, was not consulted about the use of his late brother’s name and image and has expressed his “misgivings”.

The magazine’s deputy editor has defended the use of the picture, insisting that other members of the Hitchens family were consulted and gave their approval.

Christopher Hitchens, who died in 2011, was one of the country’s best-known liberal commentators and worked as foreign editor of the New Statesman, as well as writing 18 books on politics, religion and culture.

He talked about the fact that his heavy smoking and drinking were likely to have been factors in his illness, which cut short his life at the age of 62.

When his former magazine decided to launch a new gossip column, it chose to call it The Hitch, with a picture of a cigarette-smoking Hitchens.

Friends of Hitchens took to social media to describe the move as tacky and tasteless, and when Will Lloyd, the magazine’s deputy editor, hit back by saying he had the family’s permission, Peter Hitchens intervened, saying: “Well, you didn’t ask me.”

Peter Hitchens told The Telegraph: “The first I knew about it was when it popped up on Twitter. The thing that I most dislike about it is that he is portrayed as smoking, which is not necessary and a bit of a shame.

“I can say without hesitation that, if asked, I probably would have been pretty unenthusiastic. He was never a gossip columnist – it wasn’t really his kind of journalism.”

He said that, even after the row blew up, “no one from the New Statesman has contacted me by any means known to me. They might have sent someone with a cleft stick, but normal means of communication haven’t worked”.

Peter Hitchens and his elder brother had profound disagreements over politics and the existence of God, which led to a brief estrangement in the early 2000s, but were very much reconciled by the time of Christopher’s death.

Christopher was survived by his wife and three children, who are understood to have been approached by the magazine about the use of his image.

Mr Lloyd said: “We asked permission from the family to honour one of our greatest contributors by keeping his spirit alive – they said yes.”

The Telegraph understands that Peter Hitchens and Mr Lloyd had a cordial conversation on Wednesday afternoon, and that Mr Hitchens did not demand any changes from the New Statesman.

The magazine is now expected to hold an internal discussion about whether to remove the cigarette from the cartoon of Hitchens.

data.title: AstraZeneca stokes fears of London exit with New York listing
data.description:
AstraZeneca has announced plans to list shares on the New York Stock Exchange, stoking fears that Britain’s biggest drugmaker could quit London.

In a shake-up announced on Monday, the FTSE pharmaceuticals giant said it would upgrade its US listing to ensure it is equally as important in New York as it is on the London Stock Exchange.

Bosses said the move would make it easier for the company to raise money from US investors, describing it as “a global listing for global investors in a global company”.

As part of the announcement, the company said it would maintain its London listing and remain headquartered in the UK.

However, this move will stoke fears over AstraZeneca’s future in London. Analysts at AJ Bell said it hinted “at the possibility of a more dramatic shift at some point in the future”.

Mark Kelly, the chief executive of corporate advisory firm MKP Advisors, said he expected AstraZeneca would leave the London market within the next two or three years, adding: “This is a halfway house, it’s potentially a backdoor route to an eventual full US listing.

“I don’t think anyone is necessarily surprised to see AstraZeneca going this way.”

Ketan Patel, a fund manager at AstraZeneca shareholder Whitefriars, said the risk of the company quitting London was a “concern for the UK market”. He said its exit would come as a “huge blow” to UK investors.

Such concerns will no doubt sound alarm bells for the Labour Government, which is currently trying to rally support among businesses at its party conference in Liverpool.

AstraZeneca, which was the UK’s most valuable listed company up until last week, has long been viewed as one of the businesses most likely to ditch its London listing.

Earlier this year, reports suggested that Sir Pascal Soriot, AstraZeneca’s chief executive, was keen to move the company’s main listing to New York – owing to growing frustration with the UK.

Sir Pascal described the drugmaker as a “very American company”.

The business was formed in 1999 through the merger of Sweden’s Astra AB and Britain’s Zeneca PLC. It remains headquartered in Cambridge.

Mr Kelly said: “AstraZeneca is only really here because when they did the original AstraZeneca merger, it made most sense at the time – London was a bastion of financial equity markets, and it was a wonderful place for a massive company to be listed. It’s difficult to make that argument in 2025.”

Mr Patel said: “There is a clear strategy towards a business that is US-focused, and I would not be surprised if a full exit from London is on the cards. The company already reports and pays dividends in US dollars.”

Currently, more than 40pc of AstraZeneca’s revenue comes from the US, making it the company’s largest market by a significant margin. China accounts for 13pc of revenues, while Europe stands at 21pc.

In recent months, AstraZeneca has been stepping up investment in the US – including a $50bn (£37bn) announcement – following demands from Donald Trump, the US president, for companies to invest more there.

At the same time, the pharmaceutical company has pulled a series of investments from Britain amid an ongoing row with ministers over high medicine charges.

Earlier this month, AstraZeneca halted a £200m investment in its Cambridge research facility after the Government walked away from talks to lower drug charges for medicines in the NHS.

This has fuelled claims that Britain is becoming “uninvestable” for drugmakers.

The Cambridge move followed an earlier decision by AstraZeneca to walk away from a £450m investment in a Liverpool vaccine factory in protest over the Government reducing funding for the site.

Sean Conroy, an analyst at Shore Capital, said the latest move from AstraZeneca could arguably be “a lever and a negotiation tactic to be able to demonstrate that it is cosying up to the US a bit more” – putting Britain under pressure at the same time.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: AstraZeneca threatens to stop investing in Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca’s UK chairman has threatened to halt investment in Britain unless the Government backs down over an NHS drug pricing row.

Shaun Grady warned that Britain was in danger of disappearing from a list of attractive global destinations unless it brings its “appalling” drug pricing regime into the 21st century.

Speaking at the Conservative Party conference, Mr Grady said the UK “fell down” on adopting innovation with patients being denied cutting-edge drug treatments as a result.

He blamed a lack of government support for new projects and an agreement that places a cap on how much the NHS spends on branded medicines for putting future investment at risk.

AstraZeneca announced last month that it was putting on hold a planned £200m upgrade of its Cambridge research site. The company had earlier axed a £450m expansion of its vaccine manufacturing plant in Liverpool after Labour cut funding for the project.

Mr Grady said: “We’ve [the UK] failed to keep pace on medicine investment over the years.”

Asked what it would take for the company to go ahead with the Cambridge investment, he said: “Unless the commercial environment changes, the UK is not going to be on the list.”

Mr Grady added that the company would “revisit and reflect” on its Cambridge plans as talks with the Government continue.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers this summer after failing to strike a new pricing deal on medicines supplied to the NHS. It is understood that talks are still ongoing, led by Lord Vallance, the science minister.

While Mr Grady said AstraZeneca remained a significant investor in the UK, he said it was missing out on vital investment because of an antiquated system used by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) to determine which treatments are available on the NHS that has largely remained the same since 1999.

This is determined by a formula which looks at drug effectiveness in terms of how much it would cost to give a person a year of healthy life.

“The thresholds have not been changed for 25 years, which is pretty appalling,” said Mr Grady.

He added that, unlike the UK, countries including Ireland and Singapore were offering companies significant subsidies or tax breaks to invest there. He also compared Britain with the US, where the company announced in July it would invest £50bn by 2030.

“This is a competitive global pitch,” said Mr Grady. “We announced a £50bn investment in the US. We met the governor of Virginia in our offices in London, we announced that £50bn investment 33 days later. We need to think about that speed and what truly is a concierge service.”

Speaking on the same panel, Lord Harrington – who led a review on investment under the last Tory government – said a lack of joined-up thinking was also stymieing growth.

He said: “There’s a room full of civil servants in the Department of Health whose job it is to basically kick the c--- out of pharmaceutical companies to get the price that the government pays for drugs as low as possible. They are not allowed to consider holistically the effect of those decisions on investment in a country, whereas a company clearly does.”

The decision by AstraZeneca, the UK’s largest listed company, means all of its £650m investment package announced in 2024 risks being shelved.

The Department of Health launched a review of so-called NHS rebates earlier this year under pressure from Donald Trump, the US president, who has demanded that pharmaceutical companies lower their drug prices in the US – where they are much higher than the UK and Europe.

A voluntary agreement currently places a cap on how much the NHS spends on branded medicines as part of efforts to keep costs down.

The total bill is allowed to rise by a certain percentage each year. If the NHS spends more than expected on branded medicines, it is able to claw some of that money back in rebates on drugs.

The Government raised the rate unexpectedly to almost 23pc for 2025 for newer medicines at the end of last year.

Pharmaceutical bosses have warned the scheme is preventing the launch of cutting-edge medicines in the UK. They have pressed the UK to cut the rate of rebates into single digits, a level seen elsewhere in Europe.

HS2 will almost double long-distance rail capacity between London and the West Midlands, the Government has said, and that this should in turn free up space on existing lines for more passenger and freight services.

It added that work is ongoing on possible changes to service pattern, infrastructure and rolling stock to eke out additional capacity, especially north of Birmingham, though there is no question of reversing the decision to cancel phase 2 of HS2

A spokesman for the Department for Transport said: “No decisions have been made on HS2’s future timetable, so any suggestion of reduced capacity is pure speculation.”

A Government spokesman said: “Through the Life Sciences Sector Plan, we are making the UK the destination of choice for business, investing up to £600 million in the Health Data Research Service with the Wellcome Trust, committing up to £520m to the Life Sciences Innovative Manufacturing Fund and welcoming billions of UK investment from companies like Moderna and BioNTech.

“We will always put patients and taxpayers first, encouraging investment and innovation whilst ensuring best value for money. 

“Having made an unprecedented £1bn through the Voluntary Scheme on branded medicines to industry, we will continue to engage.”

data.title: Britain more reliant than ever on foreign electricity imports
data.description:
Britain is more reliant than ever on importing power from abroad in a blow to Ed Miliband’s plans to rely on “homegrown energy”, new figures slow.

Electricity imported from neighbouring countries such as France, via undersea interconnectors, accounted for a record 16pc of consumption in the first nine months of this year.

That was up from 15.6pc over the same period last year, according to analysis by the Nuclear Industry Association (NIA).

July saw the highest monthly figure on record, when imports made up 19.5pc of the mix.

The figures come after Mr Miliband pledged to overhaul the electricity system so that domestically generated wind and solar power provides the bulk of the country’s energy.

The NIA blamed the UK’s shrinking “baseload capacity” from nuclear power – which is much lower than that of France – for driving UK power prices higher and pushing up imports.

Because the flow of interconnectors is dictated by price, higher prices in Britain mean that the country is a net importer most of the time.

According to International Energy Agency (IEA) data, the average electricity price in the UK in the year to Oct 19 was about €98 (£85) per megawatt hour, compared to €69 per megawatt hour in France.

Sam Richards, of campaign group Britain Remade, said: “If Britain is ever going to achieve energy security based on domestic supplies we must make it quicker, easier and cheaper to build the low-carbon power sources we need – from new nuclear to offshore wind and solar. 

“That means sweeping away the regulatory barriers that hold back investment, cutting the time it takes to approve projects, and putting in place a planning system fit for the scale of the challenge. 

“Without urgent reform, Britain will fall further behind and households and businesses will continue to pay the price.”

Labour ministers are under pressure to tackle Britain’s sky-high energy prices, with Rachel Reeves said to be considering cutting VAT on green levies on bills. Mr Miliband, the Energy Secretary, on Sunday hinted that a VAT cut was being actively considered by the Government.

He also told BBC One’s Sunday with Laura Kuenssberg programme: “There is only one route to get bills down, which is to go for clean power, home-grown, clean energy, that we control, so we’re not at the behest of the petrol states and the dictators.”

UK’s generating capacity dropping

Tom Greatrex, the chief executive of the Nuclear Industry Association, said: “Restoring our lost nuclear capacity is vital to UK energy security, so the Government is right to move ahead with Sizewell C and Rolls-Royce small modular reactors.”

The NIA analysis was based on monthly electricity data published by the National Energy System Operator.

Higher imports came as Britain’s own generating capacity continued to drop, with the country’s sky-high electricity prices ensuring that it was often a net importer from Europe.

Official data show the total amount of generating capacity available fell 3pc to 71.7 gigawatts last year, following the closure of Ratcliffe-on-Soar, Britain’s last coal-fired power station.

Meanwhile, separate figures from the IEA show that British power production in 2024 was 16pc lower than a decade earlier.

The Government has insisted that falling demand for power is a continuation of a trend dating back to the 1970s, caused by “improvements in energy efficiency and a move from traditional manufacturing to higher-value processes”.

However, critics have argued that the figures also reflect Britain’s shrinking industrial base.

An analysis of the UK emissions trading scheme, commissioned by Nick Timothy, the Conservative energy spokesman, on Monday suggested that up to 15pc of the reduction in the country’s carbon emissions over the past two decades can be directly attributed to factory closures.

A Government spokesman said: “The best way to strengthen Britain’s energy security is with our clean energy superpower mission, which is why we are delivering record investment in clean, homegrown power which we control.

“We are also ending the no-nuclear status quo and entering a golden age of nuclear with the biggest building programme in a generation.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Soaring demand for gold sparks unexpected rise in retail sales
data.description:
Rising demand for gold helped drive retail sales to a three-year high last month, delivering a small boost for Rachel Reeves ahead of the Budget.

Sales across Britain’s high streets rose by 0.5pc in September according to the Office for National Statistics (ONS), more than double market expectations of 0.2pc.

It marks the best month for British retailers since July 2022, with the increase largely fuelled by shoppers buying gold.

The precious metal experienced its strongest rally since the 1970s in recent months, repeatedly breaking new records.

Nervous investors typically flock to gold during times of geopolitical turmoil owing to its status as a safe-haven asset. 

The recent rally has been fuelled by concerns about a possible bubble in artificial intelligence (AI) stocks, tensions between the US and both China and Russia, and fears about the health of the $3tn (£2.2tn) private credit market.

Gold prices have risen by 50pc so far this year and doubled since the end of 2023.

More broadly, the climb has in part been fuelled by central banks, with both China and Russia significantly increasing reserves in recent years. More investors have also piled into gold since Vladimir Putin invaded Ukraine and Donald Trump took the world on the brink of a global trade war.

The ONS said: “A notable contributor to this rise was online jewellers reporting a strong demand for gold.”

The gold rally cooled earlier this week with prices suffering their biggest fall in five years. This would not have fed into the latest ONS figures for September.

Consumer confidence improving

As well as strong demand for gold jewellery, the ONS said September sales were also boosted by computers and telecommunications.

Growth in sales volumes were also revised up for August from 0.5pc to 0.6pc, although they remain 1.6pc lower than pre-Covid.

The run of positive figures suggests a minor recovery could be underway.

Matt Swannell, chief economic advisor to the EY ITEM Club, said: “Consumer confidence has improved over the past year and there appears to be scope for households to mitigate some of the impact of weaker real income growth by saving less.

“As a result, we anticipate modest growth in retail sales next year.”

Improving retail sales will be helpful for the Chancellor as she struggles to kickstart growth. Households account for the largest chunk of spending across the economy at just under two thirds of all expenditure.

However, economists warn the momentum could fade if Ms Reeves raises taxes in next month’s Budget, when she will be tasked with filling a hole in the public finances of around £30bn. The Chancellor is said to be considering breaking her manifesto pledge by raising income tax, something that would directly hit the spending power of households.

Alex Kerr from Capital Economics said: “Against a backdrop of weak employment, high inflation and with tax rises on the horizon, we doubt the retail sector will be able to sustain this strength.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Mortgage increase calculator: find out how much your bills could rise
data.description:
This tool shows how much a new mortgage deal will impact your monthly payments.

Mortgage increase calculator

How to use this calculator

Enter the amount you have left to pay on your mortgage, the number of years you have left in which to pay it, your annual pre-tax income (you can enter your household income if more than one person contributes to the mortgage) and the interest rate on your current mortgage deal.

Then, using the slider, select the interest rate for your new mortgage deal.

The results will show you your current and future mortgage payments, the difference between and two, and the proportion of your take-home pay that will be taken up by these mortgage payments.

How it works

The calculator uses the information you provide about your remaining loan, mortgage term and interest rate to work out your monthly payments.

The difference between your old and new mortgage deal is picked out so you can see how much your bills will increase, and each figure is set against your monthly income to show how much is being taken up by mortgage bills alone.

What is this calculator useful for?

This is useful for anyone who needs to remortgage and wants to know how their monthly bills will change. If your bills are set to change dramatically, you can at least start planning ahead of time.

If you’re trying to choose between more than one deal, you can easily change the information to see how your monthly payments would differ, and whether you could free up more of your take home pay.

What’s happening to mortgage rates?

While mortgage rates had gradually been tracking down in accordance with Bank Rate cuts, they’ve ticked up slightly in recent weeks. Experts say this is because the markets are now not expecting any further rate cuts this year, after hoping for one more before the start of 2026. 

The Bank of England reduced interest rates to 4pc in August, but recent inflation figures complicate the question over when the next rate cut might be. The Consumer Prices Index (CPI) measure of inflation rose to 3.8pc in September, meaning it’s spent three months in a row stuck at the same level – well ahead of the Bank’s 2pc target.

data.title: Starmer pledges to spend billions more on drugs in NHS overhaul
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer is preparing to boost NHS spending on drugs to prevent an exodus of investment from the UK.

The Government is set to overhaul a decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money. The move will allow the NHS to pay billions of pounds more for medicines.

Several drug companies have scrapped investments in the UK amid a row over how much the Government was spending on new drugs compared to other countries.

The pricing shake-up will appease drugmakers – but it also threatens to put Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, on a collision course with Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, over how to fund the multibillion-pound bill.

Lord Vallance, the science minister – who is negotiating a new deal with the industry on behalf of the Government – admitted last month that price increases were necessary to stop a wave of investment leaving the UK.

The Times reported that the Treasury is insisting that spending increases must be funded through existing departmental budgets, while NHS bosses are demanding extra financial support to pay for any deal.

The looming showdown comes as the Chancellor prepares to unveil another tax-raising Budget, with economists predicting extra levies of around £30bn to balance the books.

Ms Reeves boosted health spending at the expense of other government departments in her June spending review, with the department accounting for 90pc of the extra money for public services over the next three years.

It means the NHS and other health spending is now on course to account for half of all spending on public services by the end of the decade.

Meanwhile, a string of companies including Merck, the US cancer drug manufacturer, and Eli Lily, maker of obesity jab Mounjaro, have halted investment in the UK over the past year in protest against the NHS’s drug pricing regime.

This week, AstraZeneca’s UK chairman signalled that the company was ready to scrap investment in Britain unless the Government backed down on pricing.

Shaun Grady warned that the UK was in danger of becoming unattractive for companies unless it brings its “appalling” drugs regime into the 21st century.

Pharmaceutical companies are also under pressure from Donald Trump, the US president, who has escalated tensions by threatening 100pc tariffs on the sector if it does not invest in America.

The antiquated system used by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) to determine which treatments are available on the NHS has not been changed since 1999.

The system uses a formula which seeks to determine value for money based on a drug’s effectiveness.

Currently, Nice will approve medicines that cost less than £20,000-£30,000 for every extra “quality-adjusted life year”.

A new range of between £25,000-£35,000 per year is said to be one option under consideration.

However, the Association of the British Pharmaceutical Industry has warned that the threshold would be closer to £56,000 if it had climbed in line with the cost of living. It has called for a range closer to £50,000.

A government spokesman said: “The pharmaceutical sector and the innovative medicines it produces are critical to our NHS, our economy and the plan for change.

“Through our life sciences sector plan, we’ve committed to working with industry to accelerate growth in spending on innovative medicines compared to the previous decade.

“We’ve secured a landmark economic partnership with the US that includes working together on pharmaceutical exports from the UK whilst improving conditions for pharmaceutical companies here.

“We’re now in advanced discussions with the US administration to secure the best outcome for the UK, reflecting our strong relationship and the opportunities from close partnership with our pharmaceutical industry.”

data.title: We paid millions for our flats and there’s not enough hot water for a bath
data.description:
Two women are claiming £2m in compensation because their central London apartments allegedly do not have enough hot water for a shower or bath.

Claire Lamb and Molly Ball paid £915,000 and £1.35m respectively for their two-bedroom flats at The Belvedere in Bloomsbury.

However, they claim they have been left unable to rent, sell or live in the flats full time, due to “difficulties” with the building’s heating system.

Their daughters, who moved into the flats, said they had to shower at the gym or at work as the plumbing would deliver “short and inadequate bursts of warm water before turning cold”.

Ms Lamb and Ms Ball are now suing Regal Homes Construction, the developers, for £2m to cover the costs of fixing the problem, as well as lost rent opportunities.

But Regal is denying all fault, insisting that any drop in hot water supply should be blamed on erratic heating interface units in the flats themselves, for which the two owners are responsible.

London’s High Court heard that Ms Lamb bought her apartment in 2014, while Ms Ball bought hers in 2016, following a renovation of the Victorian terrace building.

Ms Lamb, a trained solicitor, says she started making complaints that were “too numerous to particularise” from February 2014, focusing her exasperation on a lack of heating and water “fluctuating from hot to cold”.

“She made further complaints in February 2015, March 2015, May 2015 and July 2015, where she stated that there was insufficient hot water to fill the bath and that the water issues had been going on for some time,” court documents say.

Both women say their daughters moved into the flats due to the obstacles in renting them out, but Ms Ball’s daughter Katy eventually refused to live there due to the water situation.

They maintain their apartments were “unfit for habitation” until around November 2022.

But setting out Regal’s case, barrister Rupert Cohen said: “At the core of the claimants’ case is the proposition that the heating and hot water system in the building is inadequate.

“The defence is that the central riser and central system are perfectly adequate and that, insofar as there are periods in which a flat has insufficient hot water, that is because the heating interface unit in the relevant flat is out of repair, which in turn is the responsibility of the flat owner.

“Further, their cases are predicated on an acceptance that they haven’t suffered any heating issues since November 2022.”

He added that Ms Lamb and Ms Ball have yet to come up with comprehensive expert evidence based on a “full inspection of the system”.

The full trial is set to start in 2027 with legal fees projected at around £700,000.

data.title: Energy industry prepares for net zero funding cuts
data.description:
Wind farm developers are bracing for cuts to net zero funding as Ed Miliband comes under pressure to reduce energy bills.

Offshore wind companies have been told the Energy Secretary won’t buy power “at any price” amid concern that Labour’s clean power plan risks piling costs on households.

Ministers are expected to announce a budget for the upcoming allocation round seven (AR7) of subsidies as soon as Monday, setting out how much they are prepared to spend on renewable energy projects this year.

The auction will award contracts for difference (CfDs), which guarantee renewable generators fixed revenues and are ultimately paid for by households and businesses.

AR7’s projects would be delivered in 2030 and early 2031, making it one of the last chances Mr Miliband has to secure enough capacity to achieve his goal of shifting the electricity system to renewable power by 2030.

But growing tension within Labour over the potential impact on consumers has raised the prospect that Mr Miliband may be forced to choose between lowering bills and achieving his mission.

The pressure on Mr Miliband ramped up further on Thursday, when Sir Tony Blair’s think tank published a report calling on the Energy Secretary to scrap his clean power mission in favour of one aimed at achieving “cheap power”.

At the same time, it was reported that Sir Keir Starmer was also prepared to miss the Government’s target in order to keep down consumer bills.

Downing Street later insisted the report was “completely wrong” and said the Prime Minister was sticking by the policy.

Miliband: ‘We won’t buy at any price’

However, the Government is preparing to take a tougher stance on value for money when it comes to renewable energy. 

Sources close to Mr Miliband pointed to a speech he delivered to industry last week, when he said: “We won’t buy at any price. And if specific technologies aren’t competitive, we will look elsewhere.

“We will take the long-term decisions to secure the right amount of capacity at the right price for the country.”

The strike price for offshore wind in last year’s subsidies auction was around £85 per megawatt hour, in 2024 prices, and one wind industry source said there was political pressure for developers to propose lower prices in 2025.

However, the source warned that this looked increasingly difficult as inflation and higher than expected interest rates put upward pressure on project costs.

They added: “Some developers are s- - - ing themselves because they don’t think [lower prices] is doable.”

Another wind industry source dismissed “noise” about the upcoming auction but admitted developers were “conscious that this has to be seen to be good value”.

“People recognise it needs to be cost-effective for the economy,” they said.

Adam Bell, a former government energy official and consultant at Stonehaven, said he believed £80 to £85 per megawatt hour would be seen as reasonably good value. That is around the level that many energy industry insiders believe would keep bills roughly where they are or lower them, assuming that gas prices do not rise.

Some developers may simply bow out of the auction if they couldn’t meet it, Mr Bell said.

Securing good prices is important because the AR7 contracts “lock in” prices for 20 years that influence the electricity market.

Mr Bell added: “If the Government is firm and keeps prices down, that will actually ensure a pathway for clean power because to maintain support, it has to be done on a cost-effective basis.”

Claire Coutinho, the Conservative shadow energy secretary, urged Mr Miliband to “cancel his botched wind auction before he locks us into two decades of sky-high prices”.

She said: “We’ve been warning that his approach of telling multimillion-pound wind developers that he had to buy whatever they were selling would send prices through the roof. This isn’t just what we’ll be paying, it’s what our children will be paying.”

In its election manifesto, Labour promised it would cut household energy bills by £300 a year by 2030.

Last week, energy companies warned that bills were on course to rise by 20pc instead – even if gas prices halved – owing to the mountain of green levies, network charges and system costs that are gradually being piled on to consumers.

data.title: Britain must not sell its free press to those who would silence it
data.description:
Britain’s free press is one of the crown jewels of our democracy. For centuries, it has held power to account, exposed wrongdoing, and given voice to the voiceless. Once compromised, it cannot easily be restored. That is why the proposed sale of The Telegraph to RedBird Capital Partners should trouble every person who values Britain’s sovereignty and our freedom of expression.

What may at first appear to be an ordinary commercial transaction is, on closer examination, a matter of profound national interest. RedBird Capital’s chairman, John Thornton, has cultivated an extraordinary network of relationships within the Chinese Communist Party including with senior figures in the Politburo. Last year he was photographed meeting Cai Qi, one of Xi Jinping’s closest allies and a Politburo Standing Committee member who has just been linked to the Westminster spy scandal.

Following the collapse of the Westminster spy case and the clear threat that China poses to the UK, should one of our leading newspapers fall under the influence of a foreign power that rejects those very principles?

In July this year, King’s Counsel Tom Cross and Joseph Lavery delivered a legal opinion of exceptional clarity. They concluded that, on the evidence available, the Secretary of State is legally obliged to issue a Foreign State Intervention Notice under section 70A of the Enterprise Act 2002. That step would compel the Competition and Markets Authority to investigate fully whether the proposed takeover would allow a foreign power in this case, the People’s Republic of China to control or influence the editorial policy of The Telegraph.

This is not optional. The law is explicit: where reasonable grounds exist to suspect such influence, the Secretary of State must act. And those grounds, are, I believe overwhelming.

Mr Thornton serves, and has long served, in senior roles within Chinese state-linked institutions from the Industrial and Commercial Bank of China and Lenovo Group to the China Investment Corporation, Beijing’s sovereign wealth fund. He has chaired organisations intertwined with the CCP’s political advisory apparatus, publicly praised Xi Jinping, and received honours from the Chinese Government itself.

RedBird has co-invested with Tencent, a company designated by the US Department of Defence as a “Chinese military company”. The firm has also opened a regional headquarters in Hong Kong, where press freedom has been brutally dismantled under the National Security Law. This includes the appalling trial and incarceration of Jimmy Lai, the owner of Apple Daily.

Taken together, these connections create a pattern which should inform any decision on ownership. The legal threshold for suspicion is intentionally low; Parliament designed it so that action could be taken before influence turned into control.

Some may argue that concerns like these are alarmist or protectionist, I don’t believe that is correct. They are rooted in the hard-learnt lessons of recent years: the infiltration of British universities, technology partnerships, and strategic industries by actors aligned with the Chinese Communist Party. In each case, influence began subtly: a donation here, a partnership there, until dependence became the norm.

RedBird’s bid for The Telegraph, I believe, is more than a business deal. It is a test of Britain’s resolve to defend its own institutions. The United States has already recognised the strategic reach of Chinese corporate influence; it is time Britain did the same. A foreign power should never be allowed sway over independent journalism.

I hope the Secretary of State will act by issuing a Foreign State Intervention Notice (FSIN). This is not a political decision; it is the lawful protection of our democracy.

Our freedom of the press was not won cheaply, and it cannot be sold off to the highest bidder. I hope the Government will agree with me and many others in the industry, and block this acquisition, making it clear that Britain’s press freedoms are a precious guardian of our personal freedoms too.

data.title: Why finding a builder, gardener or cleaner has become a competitive sport
data.description:
Emma Horton has been “ghosted” by two cleaners in the past year.

“One seemed really keen, came a couple of times but then never returned and stopped responding to messages,” says Horton, 48, who lives in Cambridgeshire and owns a PR company. “Another did a similar thing, while the third kept cancelling last minute for various different reasons, each one more far-fetched than the last, so I let her go. I’ve given up trying to find one.”

Call it a first-world problem but finding someone to clean your house seems more difficult than ever these days.

Several times a week, my local area WhatsApp group, which numbers almost 650 people across Essex and Suffolk, pings with a variation on the same message: “Help! Can anyone recommend a good cleaner?”

It’s not only cleaners. Sourcing gardeners is a problem, too – just ask the King. Staff shortages, low pay and the monarch’s exacting demands have reportedly led to an exodus from Highgrove House, his Gloucestershire residence. Since 2022, 11 out of 12 garden staff have left, including two head gardeners.

And then there’s the battle for builders and tradesmen. Often, people report trying to get quotes from as many as seven or eight builders – in many cases, they never hear back and, if they do, the quotes are often eye-wateringly expensive.

According to the latest state of trade survey by the Federation of Master Builders (FMB), 61pc of businesses report being affected by skilled labour shortages. This in combination with rising materials prices and increased wage and National Insurance costs means that 61pc of companies surveyed by the FMB have also increased the prices they charge for their work this year.

“Since Covid and Brexit, households have found themselves competing for cleaners, gardeners and tradesmen in a way they simply didn’t have to before,” says Robin Edwards, of London buying agency Curetons.

“It was a double whammy, as Covid accelerated people leaving the workforce and changed work preferences, while Brexit reduced the pool of migrant labour that had previously supplied many domestic service roles.”

The recruitment crisis means that, in many parts of the country, securing household staff can feel like a competitive sport, with homeowners increasingly willing to fight dirty.

“In the Cotswolds, we have seen people offering cleaners cash on the spot to jump ship from a neighbour, while village politics have also erupted into full-blown feuds over the loyalty of a particularly talented gardener,” says Harry Gladwin, of buying agency The Buying Solution.

“Increasingly, success in the countryside is measured not just by the splendour of your house but by whether you have managed to hold on to the cleaner who knows how to coax red wine out of a Cotswold stone floor.”

A homeowner from Essex says the cleaning company she used would often cancel just before the cleaners were due. “This was usually because the manager had received a call from the council for an emergency clean or even from the police for specialist forensic cleaning, which was presumably better paid,” she says. “I clean after the cleaners have been but feel I have to keep them on as it’s better than nothing.”

According to official data from the Office for National Statistics, the number of people employed as “cleaners and domestics” has actually risen over the past year or so. After dropping sharply from 491,800 in 2021 to 460,300 in 2022, it recovered last year to 492,200.

Crucially, however, the survey only picks up people who work for employers registered for PAYE and VAT. As such, many will likely be cleaning commercial premises such as schools and hospitals, and the numbers will not reflect the thousands of cleaners who work mainly for private households being paid in cash.

And this is where the worst shortages are to be found. The British Cleaning Council (BCC) says recruiting cleaners is particularly difficult in London, where 60pc of the industry workforce was born overseas. Other regions have historically relied much less heavily on migrant labour – in the North East of England, for instance, only 4pc of those working in cleaning were born overseas, the BCC says.

“We met a new client recently who has just lost their trusted Bulgarian cleaner of many years as she has decided to move back home – and we’re hearing this more and more,” says Simmi Sangha, of property company Eccord, which manages 150 homes across London.

“Those who are staying are increasing their hourly rates and most clients are paying without hesitation because they don’t want to risk losing them.”

While the most recent ONS figures, dating from April 2024, showed that the median pay for those employed full-time as cleaners was £12.12 an hour, anecdotal evidence suggests households are paying much more than this – in London and the South East £25 an hour is common. With some cleaners in the capital now reportedly running waiting lists for clients, homeowners are going to increasingly desperate measures to attract and keep them.

“Lavish birthday and Christmas presents and bonuses are now common,” Edwards says. “I know of one client who lent their housekeeper the free use of their villa in Portugal for a week and another, an aesthetic doctor, who gives his cleaner free Botox.”

There’s also a recruitment crisis in horticulture, with the number of gardeners and landscape gardeners falling by 29,000 between 2019 and 2021, according to the ONS’s labour force survey.

The sector relied heavily on seasonal workers from the EU before Brexit, while people in their 50s and 60s are retiring and are not being replaced by new apprentices. Fewer young people are willing to embark on a career that’s often hard work but poorly paid, despite the extensive knowledge needed for skilled roles.

Qualified self-employed gardeners will charge an average rate of between £30 and £41 an hour, according to research by the Gardeners Guild, although this assumes you book them for a full day and are a regular customer. Irregular customers may expect to pay more.

In some cases, they face paying significantly more. A client of Eccord in Chelsea, south-west London, was recently quoted £600 plus VAT per month for a gardener to come in once a fortnight to do a basic tidy up of a small terraced rear garden and a few window boxes.

Meanwhile, Jennie Hancock, of the West Sussex buying agency Property Acquisitions, has a client who bought a house and paid a “significant premium” to persuade the gardener to stay on. “This wasn’t only because he was excellent and knew the garden inside out but he’d also worked on the Capability Brown gardens at nearby Petworth Park, which brought a certain cachet,” she says.

And they’re the lucky ones. It took Katie Neale, from the buying agency Haringtons UK, five attempts before she could even get a gardener to reply to her requests to come and work at her own rural home. “When he finally came, after an hour of wandering around with his hands on his hips, he declared the garden ‘a life’s work’ and promised a quote that never materialised,” she says. “I now see him every week at my neighbour’s house, where he ducks behind the hedges whenever I drive past.”

And don’t get Emma Horton started on builders. She and her husband would like to knock a wall through from their kitchen to the dining room, add bifold windows in the kitchen and put a utility room into part of the garage. Yet, just as for cleaners, she’s been ghosted by two builders and told by a third that he can’t do the job for another year.

“We have another company coming round and I am hopeful that we might get somewhere, but the process is so frustrating and time-consuming,” she says. “I wonder whether it’s because it’s not a particularly huge job, but I wish they would just respond, even if it’s to turn us down.”

Jeremy Gray, of the FMB, says it has long been tricky to get a builder as construction has never kept up with demand. “Good ones are booked up 12 to 18 months ahead and that has always been the case – if anyone said they could start next week, I’d run a mile,” he says. “Cost pressures have become more pronounced since Covid particularly, and builders are a lot better paid than people realise.”

And spare a thought for the wealthy, who are also battling for staff to look after their houses and gardens. The 2025 Estate Staff Salary Survey by Knight Frank estate agency, released in June, found that 87pc of estates and country houses said it was becoming harder to attract good staff, while almost 60pc said retaining workers was also becoming more difficult.

This comes despite years of steady wage increases. The average salary of an estate director has risen from £83,224 in 2020 to £103,221; a housekeeper’s pay has gone from £24,292 to £30,426; and a head gardener’s income has risen from £28,806 to £36,428, according to Knight Frank.

There are several factors to blame, including rural locations that are a barrier to recruitment, says Alastair Paul, of Knight Frank’s rural consultancy.

In the countryside, the Covid-era race for space that suddenly ratcheted up the demand for rural living was also a factor, although that has now calmed down, points out Philip Eddell, the director of Eddell & Co property consultancy.

It’s not uncommon for roles for housekeepers, gardeners and maintenance teams to sit empty for months, says Kathryn Donkin, of Strutt & Parker. “I know an estate that has been advertising for a maintenance worker for nearly a year,” she adds.

data.title: Let teenagers drive buses, ministers told
data.description:
Ministers have been urged to scrap “outdated” restrictions on teenage bus and coach drivers to ease a nationwide staffing shortage.

Bus and coach drivers under 20 are banned from operating services longer than 31 miles, despite qualified 18 and 19-year-olds being permitted to drive articulated lorries or petrol tankers with no distance limit.

Now the Confederation of Passenger Transport (CPT) has called on the Government to “unlock the potential of young people”.

Restrictions on teenage bus and coach drivers exclude them from the vast majority of coach work, as well as many bus routes outside major towns and cities.

The CPT said the limit was hampering recruitment. It stated that the proportion of unfilled coach and bus driver roles in the UK was 12.4 per cent and 3.4 per cent respectively, resulting in a total shortfall of more than 4,000 drivers.

‘I wish I could have started sooner’

Keith McNally, CPT operations director, said: “The evidence is clear: age itself is not a key determinant of collision risk – experience is.

“Department for Transport (DfT) and Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency data show that young vocational drivers have fewer collisions than their peers with car licences, and perform comparably with drivers aged 21 and over.”

Aidan Travasso, who turned 20 earlier this month, became a driver for Swindon-based Barnes Coaches when he was 18 and was disappointed to discover he could not fulfil most of the company’s contracts.

He said: “It didn’t make sense. My driving ability didn’t change between the day before and the day after my 20th birthday.

“Now I can drive longer routes, it’s much more fun. I just wish I could have started sooner.”

The DfT announced in May it will lower the minimum age for train drivers from 20 to 18 to ease staff shortages.

A consultation on a proposal to take similar action for bus and coach drivers was conducted by the Conservative government last year, but no outcome has been published.

‘Remove these outdated restrictions’

The CPT also warned that “many willing applicants drift to other sectors” because they could not start training until they had received their provisional licence, which “often takes weeks or months”.

The body called for this requirement to be dropped.

Graham Vidler, CPT chief executive, said: “We all want safe, reliable and affordable bus and coach services. The quickest way to support that is to unlock the potential of young people who are eager to start their careers in our sector.

“The Government has already taken this step for rail – now it’s time to back Britain’s buses and coaches by removing these outdated restrictions.”

Richard Holden, the shadow transport secretary, said scrapping the 30-mile limit would “open up new opportunities for young drivers, strengthen the coach and bus sectors, and get more services running in rural communities”.

A Department for Transport spokesman said: “Better buses are central to the Government’s Plan for Change, and after decades of decline, we’re providing £1 billion in funding this year to improve the reliability and frequency of bus services across the country through our landmark Bus Services Bill.”

data.title: Lynton Crosby returns to lead lobbying group after chief departs
data.description:
Political strategist Sir Lynton Crosby has returned to running CT Group after the lobbying firm’s chief executive stepped down.

CT Group said Sir Lynton, one of its co-founders and the architect of successive Conservative election victories, would once again oversee business operations on a day-to-day basis.

The business also announced the exit of Jon de Jager, who replaced Sir Lynton in early 2024 as its first non-founder chief executive in its 23-year history.

CT Group said Mr de Jager, a former EY partner who had been with CT Group for nine years, resigned as the company prepared for its “next chapter of growth”.

Sir Lynton, who is already executive chairman, said the company was “positioned to pursue growth in new markets and capitalise on new offerings, while ensuring established markets continue their growth trajectory”.

It has recently been expanding further into the Middle East and Asia, with new offices in Abu Dhabi and Singapore.

Sir Lynton is expected to work with its managing directors to steer the business as it considers permanent changes to its leadership over the next few weeks.

His return comes after he signalled he was stepping away from frontline politics in early 2024, having spearheaded a series of election campaigns for Boris Johnson, Theresa May and David Cameron.

Sir Lynton co-founded CT Group in 2002, advising Conservative parties in Britain, Canada and Australia. It has also carried out advisory work for coal traders, tobacco companies and property firms.

Other CT employees include figures such as David Canzini – formerly deputy chief of staff under Mr Johnson – and Mark Fullbrook, previously Liz Truss’s chief of staff.

Last year, the group appointed Tory peer Baroness Helena Morrissey as a non-executive director.

In 2015, Sir Lynton – nicknamed the “Wizard of Oz” – was credited with helping to steer Mr Cameron to a resounding victory at the election. He was knighted in the New Year’s Honours list later that year.

Sir Lynton later acted as an informal adviser for Mr Johnson during his time as Prime Minister, having previously worked together in the 2012 London Mayoral election.

His protégée Isaac Levido ran Rishi Sunak’s election campaign in 2024, which ultimately saw the Conservatives defeated by Sir Keir Starmer’s Labour Party.

data.title: Musk attacks ‘corporate terrorists’ for voting against his $1tn pay deal
data.description:
Elon Musk has branded shareholder proxy advisers as “corporate terrorists” after they opposed his $1tn (£750bn) pay package.

The Tesla chief executive said Glass Lewis and ISS had “no freaking clue” after both advisers urged shareholders to reject his pay package at the car company’s annual meeting next month.

Under the proposed scheme, Mr Musk would be in line to receive millions of shares if he turns Tesla from a $1tn company into an $8.5tn business within the next 10 years.

Mr Musk would be awarded a $1tn payday if he hits all his targets, including selling one million AI robots and having one million self-driving robotaxis on the road. This would make Mr Musk, who is already the world’s richest person, the world’s first trillionaire.

Glass Lewis and ISS have both advised Tesla shareholders to vote against the package at the company’s meeting on Nov 6.

Glass Lewis this week claimed the proposed payment terms should “warrant significant concern” as they would dilute other shareholders’ stakes, potentially giving Mr Musk a “materially increased ownership stake”.

Glass Lewis said it also could hand the Tesla boss “extraordinary pay levels without commensurately exceptional performance”.

However, Mr Musk claimed he did not view the package as “compensation”, telling investors late on Wednesday that it was designed to help bolster his voting power at the company.

Mr Musk said: “I just don’t feel comfortable building a robot army here and then being ousted because of some asinine recommendations from ISS and Glass Lewis.”

The Tesla board has previously warned that Mr Musk could walk away from the business if shareholders do not approve the package.

The latest development follows years of scrutiny over the Tesla chief’s pay awards.

Last year, a judge struck down a previous pay plan from 2018, which suggested paying Mr Musk $56bn. At the time, it was one of the largest payouts in US corporate history. Mr Musk hit his targets for that plan several years early.

In September, Robyn Denholm, the Tesla chairman, said: “Simply put, retaining and incentivising Elon is fundamental to Tesla achieving these goals and becoming the most valuable company in history.”

Writing on his social media platform X on Wednesday night, Mr Musk said the companies should be forced to register as “investment advisers”, saying: “The absurdity, arguably illegality, of them not doing so is obvious in the description.”

Investment advisers face more regulatory scrutiny than proxy advisers.

Mr Musk said Glass Lewis and ISS wielded an unduly large influence at companies. He added: “ISS and Glass Lewis have no actual ownership themselves and often vote along random political lines unrelated to shareholder interests.”

A Glass Lewis spokesman said: “Our job as a proxy adviser is to provide informed analysis and recommendations to our clients worldwide. Those that are Tesla shareholders will ultimately make their own decisions about Mr Musk’s pay proposal and the board directors that put it forward for shareholder vote.”

ISS did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

data.title: GSK boss says US is ‘biggest priority’ as Trump pressures drugmakers
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drugmaker has said the US is its “biggest priority” amid pressure from Donald Trump to slash prices for US citizens.

Dame Emma Walmsley, who will step down from GSK at the end of the year, praised the US and said the group is working with the Trump administration after it launched a campaign to address high drug prices.

Shares in pharmaceutical companies surged on Tuesday after rival pharma group Pfizer struck a deal with the White House to lower prices for some of its medicines. Mr Trump has also demanded greater investment in the US from drug companies after introducing a 100pc tariff on branded drugs made abroad.

The Pfizer agreement, which averts tariffs for three years, triggered a relief rally that sent shares in AstraZeneca up 11.2pc, as it reclaimed its place as Britain’s most valuable company. GSK shares jumped by 6.2pc.

Dame Emma admitted GSK was “betting big on the US” after she pledged during Mr Trump’s UK state visit last month to invest $30bn (£22bn) in America over the next five years.

She said: “Absolutely GSK is engaging constructively. The markets also view it this way, as it’s about constructive dialogue between the industry and the Government.

“The US is by far the biggest priority market for our industry because the US is the best country in the world to discover and develop innovation.”

As the country’s largest drugmaker, GSK has become a national champion for the UK pharmaceutical sector, despite generating only a small proportion of its revenues in the UK.

Britain represents just 2pc of the £65bn drugmaker sales, compared to 52pc in the US.

Despite this, Dame Emma said the group would not ditch its listing on the London stock market for New York. GSK’s shares have fallen overall in the eight years since she took over in 2017.

“I’m not getting distracted by the listing questions that everyone keeps asking about,” Dame Emma told Bloomberg’s Women, Money & Power conference.

“Our listing location doesn’t distract from where we allocate our capital in terms of investments. There are no plans for that.”

She also said there had been “zero engagement” on a potential merger with any other UK pharmaceutical company.

AstraZeneca fears

Her comments come days after AstraZeneca stoked fears that Britain’s biggest drugmaker could quit London after it announced plans to list shares on the New York Stock Exchange.

In a shake-up announced on Monday, the FTSE pharmaceuticals giant said it would upgrade its US listing to ensure it is equally as important in New York as it is on the London Stock Exchange.

Bosses said the move would make it easier for the company to raise money from US investors, describing it as “a global listing for global investors in a global company”.

Analysts at AJ Bell said the decision hinted “at the possibility of a more dramatic shift at some point in the future”.

data.title: Household electricity bills to change with the weather
data.description:
Millions of homes will see their electricity bills change with the weather after E.On announced plans to charge customers by the half hour.

German-owned E.On will begin taking electricity meter readings from customers every 30 minutes, instead of monthly as happens now.

Under the scheme, households will see electricity prices vary throughout the day. Prices will be partly linked to the levels of wind and sunshine available to power renewable generators, as well as overall demand.

For example, power prices could rise sharply on cold, windless winter evenings when wind and solar generation plummets and demand surges.

Conversely, prices could plummet on warm, windy days when power generation from wind and solar surges, but demand is low. In theory, they could even become negative, meaning suppliers would pay consumers to use their surplus power.

E.On has five million customers, making it one of the UK’s biggest suppliers. The changes will affect those with smart meters.

Octopus Energy has already experimented with a similar scheme, offering its “Agile” tariff to customers on a voluntary basis. All other suppliers are set to follow by 2027 under changes ordered by Ed Miliband.

The Energy Secretary argues the scheme is essential to the UK’s transition to green energy, but critics warn it relies on homes having a working smart meter – a goal still many years away.

The change is also intended to lower electricity usage, something that is likely to be controversial given it will impose higher prices in the early morning and evening peak periods when families are typically showering, cooking, watching TV and warming their homes.

Claire Coutinho, shadow energy minister, said: “Ed Miliband’s obsession with net zero will make Britain depend on the weather for its electricity. It means millions of homes will have to change their energy use, not when they want to, but when the wind is blowing.

“Our energy system should suit what people want to do, not the other way around. We need to bring back consumer choice and focus on making electricity cheap.”

A Department for Energy Security and Net Zero spokesman said: “Half-hourly settlements give households across the country the choice and control over when and how they use their energy.

“No one will be forced to change their tariff – instead we are empowering consumers and supporting them to bring down their bills.”

E.On said the new billing system would allow it to launch new tariffs, allowing customers to set up appliances like washing machines or water heaters to run at night when prices are usually lower.

Households with battery systems or EVs could also use the new system to charge up when power is cheapest.

Ramona Vlasiu, of E.On Next, said: “Half-hourly settlement is a building block for a smarter, fairer, more innovative energy system.

“The goal is to make electricity bills more accurate and reflective of how customers live their lives, and to empower those consumers to adapt their energy usage and switch to tariffs that allow for more flexible consumption.”

She added: “Together, these factors can help to drive down the cost of energy for people and reward them for playing a part in the future energy system.”

Simon Francis, coordinator of the End Fuel Poverty Coalition, said the energy industry should focus first on ensuring everyone has a working smart meter rather than changing the way billing works.

He said: “Millions of people have already been left behind in the smart meter rollout, and it is vital that the energy industry urgently fixes problems with existing meters and properly compensates customers for failures.

“Access to smart tariffs must be universal, and smart meter failures must not become a reason why the most vulnerable pay more for energy.”

Barnaby Wharton, of RenewableUK, a trade body for the wind farm industry, said: “By giving people more control over the way they manage their bills and providing opportunities for them to save money, we can enable the UK to make the best use of the vast amounts of electricity we’re generating”.
