data.title: Telegraph takeover may face national security investigation over China links
data.description:
The proposed takeover of The Telegraph by a consortium with links to China could face investigation as a potential national security threat, the Government has said.

Ministers were questioned in the House of Lords over the protracted attempt by RedBird Capital to engineer a £500m acquisition partly relying on the wealth of the United Arab Emirates royal family.

Asked whether the takeover would be investigated under the National Security and Investment Act, which grants ministers sweeping powers to intervene, culture minister Baroness Twycross said intervention “could be considered” if it was deemed to raise national security concerns. 

A RedBird spokesman said: “We are happy to work with any regulator or government body that wants to look into the proposed transaction.”

It threatens another hurdle for RedBird, which is attempting to push through a deal nearly two-and-a-half years after The Telegraph was first cast into limbo by the financial troubles of the Barclay family, its previous owners.

RedBird made a formal application to the Government last week to be allowed to begin the takeover. Its consortium is also expected to include stakes for the Daily Mail owner Lord Rothermere and Sir Leonard Blavatnik, the billionaire industrialist.

Under separate laws that apply only to newspaper and news website acquisitions, the Government already faces demands to investigate the impact on the public interests in plurality, free expression and accurate news. The risk of foreign state influence posed by RedBird’s plans for the UAE to become a minority shareholder are also open to regulatory scrutiny.

Baroness Twycross declined to confirm that fears of Chinese influence sparked by the longstanding links to Beijing of senior figures at RedBird would guarantee an investigation. But she added: “I’d like to reassure the noble Lord that any sale of The Telegraph will be subject to the regime.”

The Conservative Baroness Stowell said: “I do hope very much that the Secretary of State is able to say soon that she’s minded to involve the Competition and Markets Authority [CMA] and Ofcom in the scrutiny of this bid.

“Because I think it will be important, should she decide to give it the go-ahead, for our future confidence in The Telegraph’s editorial independence.”

‘They’re buying influence’

Baroness Stowell also raised concern that reliance on debt or an earn-out to the UAE could confer influence on top of its planned shareholding.

The £500m price, set in the deal between the Barclay family and the UAE, is significantly higher than most media analysts can justify using normal valuation approaches. RedBird has rejected claims it is overpaying.

Baroness Twycross replied: “We’re absolutely clear that the overall intention of the policy is that a foreign state should not have any control or influence, and this is what the Secretary of State considers carefully when she looks at the details of each case.”

RedBird, which is led by the former Goldman Sachs banker Gerry Cardinale, was involved in a prior attempt to take control of The Telegraph that was majority funded by the UAE royal family. It was blocked by the Conservatives in spring 2024 following a cross-party outcry over press freedom.

RedBird’s new consortium includes an expected 15pc stake for the UAE. The investors in what would be RedBird’s own controlling stake have not been disclosed.

However, the decades-long Chinese associations held by John Thornton, the firm’s chairman, have raised concerns that the fund may contain money under the influence of Beijing, such as billionaire family offices. Mr Thornton, another former Goldman Sachs banker, also sits on the advisory council of the China Investment Corporation, the country’s largest sovereign wealth fund.

No evidence is available to substantiate fears of Chinese money in The Telegraph takeover, but against the backdrop of the Westminster spying scandal, the Government and regulators are under increased pressure to investigate.

The Liberal Democrat peer Lord Fox said: “My Lords, as we know, a fund with Abu Dhabi money and probably Chinese money, is acquiring what in global terms is a small player, relatively small, but The Telegraph is significant in the UK.

“The best explanation for their motives that I can come up with is that they’re buying influence.”

‘No Chinese state funds involved’

A RedBird spokesman said: “A fundamental tenet of RedBird Capital Partners’ investment thesis for The Telegraph is editorial independence. Maintaining the highest standards of journalistic integrity is at the heart of RedBird’s plans to grow this iconic brand in the UK and internationally.

“There is no Chinese influence in RedBird Capital’s proposed acquisition of The Telegraph.

“There are no Chinese state funds being invested in this deal. There are no Chinese LPs in RedBird funds.”

In private equity, LPs stands for limited partners, the financial contributors to the funds that firms invest. They can be funds themselves, based anywhere.

Scrutiny of the new bid is being overseen by Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary. She claimed this week that “the last government was far too relaxed about the challenges and the threats posed by China”.

Luke de Pulford, executive director of the Inter-Parliamentary Alliance on China, which campaigns on the security threat from Beijing, said last week: “Unless Lisa Nandy intervenes, The Telegraph will be taken over by a company led by John Thornton, arguably the most pro-Beijing businessman in the whole of America.

“He even said to CCP leaders that they needed to ‘get in’ to English-speaking media. Mad.”

A group of free speech groups including Index on Censorship, Reporters Without Borders and Article 19 wrote to Ms Nandy in August, arguing that RedBird’s links with China “threaten media pluralism, transparency and information integrity in the UK”.

The Telegraph is currently effectively operating without an owner, overseen by independent directors. RedBird manages the large debt secured against it, which was intended to be converted into ownership in the original UAE-backed deal.

Special legal orders are in place to protect the newspaper’s independence of RedBird and the UAE while wrangling over its ownership continues.

Lord Fox asked: “Will the minister commit that her department will investigate any prima facie breaches of the orders protecting The Telegraph from influence by RedBird, breaches that may already have occurred? And will she commit to publishing the investigation findings?”

Baroness Twycross said she could not comment on a live takeover situation given Ms Nandy’s quasi-judicial role, which leaves her open to judicial review on any perceived breach of process. She said she would write to Lord Fox about publishing official findings on any alleged violations of the orders protecting The Telegraph.

Mr Cardinale, who is on the board of the Hollywood studio Paramount after RedBird provided $2bn for a takeover led by the billionaire Ellison family, recently said: “Don’t buy into a news organisation, whether it’s broadcast or print, if you want to influence it. That’s just bad business. Right?”

Last week he said: “RedBird is committed to partnering with the talented team at The Telegraph to help navigate this evolving landscape and ensure The Telegraph’s world class journalism and brand are positioned for success going forward.”

data.title: Evri named UK’s worst delivery company for third year running
data.description:
Evri has been named the UK’s worst delivery company for the third year running after coming under fire for persistent delays and parcels being left in the wrong place.

More than four in 10 people said they were dissatisfied with Evri’s level of service, according to new figures from Ofcom, compared to just 31pc of people who said they were satisfied.

It marks the third consecutive year that Evri has ranked at the bottom of the delivery league table, with satisfaction levels dropping further since 2024.

Scrutiny of Evri’s performance comes after it struck a deal to merge with DHL’s e-commerce business in the UK earlier this year.

The tie-up, which was approved by the competition watchdog last month, means the company will deliver more than a billion parcels each year – or around a quarter of all UK parcels.

The online shopping boom has triggered massive growth in parcel volumes in recent years. A record 4.2 billion parcels were sent and received across the UK last year, up 7pc on the previous year and eclipsing the pandemic peak in 2020-21.

However, Britons have grown increasingly frustrated with late, damaged or missing parcels as service levels struggle to keep pace with demand.

Across all operators, more than two thirds of people have experienced a delivery issue over the last six months.

The most common complaint was delivery delays, accounting for more than a quarter of issues. That was followed by parcels left in inappropriate locations.

Around a fifth of complaints related to delivery drivers not knocking loudly enough while a similar proportion related to not being given enough time to answer the door.

Other common concerns included parcels not being left in a designated safe space, drivers not attempting to deliver in person and parcels being damaged or not delivered at all.

Yodel ranked second lowest in the survey of 4,000 adults with a third of its customers saying they were dissatisfied with the company’s ability to handle complaints.

Royal Mail – which was snapped up by Czech billionaire Daniel Kretinsky in a £3.6bn deal earlier this year – ranked in the bottom half of the table, scoring a dissatisfaction level of 24pc.

While that is a small improvement on the previous year, the figures will raise alarm bells for the postal service as it shifts its focus to parcels.

‘Premier parcel delivery’

The company last month announced plans to take over thousands of convenience stores to rebrand them as hubs for collecting, sending and returning parcels.

That is in addition to the thousands of parcel lockers it is building across the country, alongside partnerships with retailers such as Sainsbury’s and Co-op.

Amazon ranked top of the survey with 57pc of respondents satisfied and just 16pc dissatisfied. This was closely followed by FedEx and UPS.

Ofcom has toughened up its rules around complaints processes for couriers in recent years in a bid to tackle concerns about the quality of service. The regulator said it was pressing delivery companies to make further, sustained improvements.

An Evri spokesman said: “Customer satisfaction is our top priority and every parcel matters to us. That is why, over the past year, we’ve invested £57mn in our operations and technology – all to make our parcel delivery service smoother, faster and more sustainable.

“From the nation’s leading retailers and marketplaces to pre-loved platforms, our partners and customers continue to trust us and the service we deliver, and our growing parcel volumes and almost four million five-star Trustpilot reviews are proof of this.

“We are set to deliver about 900 million parcels this financial year and – having completed our landmark merger with DHL UK in October – we are on track to become the UK’s premier parcel delivery company for businesses and consumers alike.”

data.title: Tax on rental income: What is it, calculations and how to pay
data.description:
In the same way that workers must pay tax on their earnings, landlords also have to pay tax on the income they receive from tenants.

While buy-to-let investors used to pay tax on rental income “net of mortgage costs”, they can now only claim a 20pc tax credit on their mortgage interest payments, which has eaten into profits.

Here, Telegraph Money explains how tax on rental income is calculated, and what it means if you have multiple properties and rentals overseas.

  • What is tax on rental income?
  • How is tax on rental income calculated?
  • Rental income tax on multiple properties
  • Rental income tax on properties overseas
  • Rental income tax FAQs

What is tax on rental income?

Tax on rental income refers to income tax, which is payable on the money you receive from tenants. The rates, thresholds and tax-free allowances are the same as for any other type of income, whether it’s from employment, or a pension income.

You can also use certain costs incurred as part of being a landlord to reduce your tax bill – but since the rules changed a few years ago, you can now offset less of your mortgage costs.

Chris Etherington, of accountants RSM UK, said: “For residential lettings, there are special provisions for mortgage and other loan finance costs. Relief for these is given as a reduction in tax, rather than set against rental profits, at a rate of 20pc.”

How is tax on rental income calculated?

Tax on rental income works differently depending on whether you are taxed as an individual, or as a company, if you have incorporated the ownership of the property.

If you are taxed as an individual, the income tax will work in much the same way as the tax you would pay on income earned from employment.

Rachael Griffin, of wealth manager Quilter, said: “It is taxed at 20pc, 40pc or 45pc, depending on your tax bracket. The main difference is that you would need to complete a self-assessment tax return and calculate your profits.”

The amount you need to pay is determined by subtracting any tax-free allowances and expenses from your total rental income – resulting in your taxable profit. 

As mentioned above, this profit is then taxed according to your income band.

For the 2025-26 tax year:

  • Basic-rate taxpayers pay 20pc on income between £12,570-£50,270
  • Higher-rate taxpayers pay 40pc on income between £50,271-£125,140
  • Additional-rate taxpayers pay 45pc on income over £125,140.

While most taxpayers pay nothing on earnings up to £12,570 – the personal allowance – this reduces once you earn more than £100,000; for every £2 extra you earn, you lose £1 of the tax-free allowance. By the time you earn £125,140, you’ll have no personal allowance at all. 

As the UK has a progressive tax system, be aware that you need to add any rent from letting a property with any other taxable income to determine the tax rate that may apply.

Ms Griffin added: “For example, if you were earning £35,000 from employment and earned a further £20,000 from rental income, you would be pushed into the higher rate of income tax and would therefore pay the higher rate of 40pc on £4,730.”

Why do you pay tax on rental income?

  • Rental income is considered taxable earnings, like a salary or business profit
  • Paying tax on rental income is a legal obligation
  • While other investments can benefit from being held in an Isa (individual savings account) where growth is tax-free, the Government does not incentivise investing in property in the same way
  • Even though the property tax rules have been put together by different governments over time, a key reason for the current tax rules could be due to the lack of housing supply. The rules in place at present could potentially deter many would-be landlords from starting – or continuing – rental businesses, in theory increasing the number of properties available for owner-occupiers.

Rental income tax on multiple properties

If you have multiple properties, you will typically need to pool together all of the profits and treat them as income from one business. You then deduct allowable expenses for each property to calculate your total taxable profit.

Nicholas Mendes, of broker John Charcol, said: “For example, if one generates a profit and another operates at a loss, the loss from one property can offset the profit from another – potentially reducing your overall tax liability.”

To make this process easier, it is highly advisable to keep separate, detailed records for each property, covering both income and expenses.

Mr Mendes added: “This ensures accuracy and simplifies the process of filing your tax return.”

Note though, that there are some exceptions to the guidelines set out above.

Mr Etherington said: “This includes rental income from properties held in partnership which would need to be declared as a separate rental business.”

Rental income tax on properties overseas

Be aware that profits from an overseas rental property can still be taxable in the UK, even if tax has been paid overseas as well.

Mr Mendes said: “Owning a property overseas adds an extra layer of complexity to your tax obligation. Fortunately, the UK has double taxation agreements with many countries, allowing you to claim relief and avoid being taxed twice on the same income.”

But as navigating the specific tax rules in both jurisdictions can be challenging, it’s worth seeking professional advice to ensure compliance – and also to make sure you don’t end up overpaying on tax.

When to pay rental income tax

The following are the deadlines for the 2024-25 return:

  • 31 October 2025: paper Self Assessment return deadline
  • 30 December 2025: deadline to submit online if you want HMRC to collect tax through PAYE code (for under £3,000 owed)
  • 31 January 2026: deadline for online Self Assessment return and balancing payment for 2024–25, plus first payment on account towards 2025–26
  • 31 July 2026: second payment on account for 2025–26

Failure to declare rental income can result in fines, penalties and in some cases legal action. By staying compliant, you can ensure your tax affairs are in order.

If you let a property and earn income from it, take care to stay within the rules. You will need to calculate profits and file a self-assessment tax return.

Expenses and tax allowances

Expenses:

Allowable expenses are costs which are incurred “wholly and exclusively” for the purposes of renting out a property. They can be deducted from your rental income to reduce your taxable profit. These include:

  • Utility bills
  • Property repairs
  • Service charge costs
  • Letting agent fees
  • Landlord insurance.

Ms Griffin said: “These expenses can help to reduce your taxable income when completing your self-assessment tax return, so it is important to keep a detailed log of all expenses.”

Allowances:

There are a host of property-related tax-free allowances for individual landlords. These include:

  • the property allowance, which can exempt up to £1,000 of property income a year from income tax
  • the rent-a-room scheme, which can allow an owner-occupier to receive up to £7,500 of rental income a year tax-free – provided it is received from a tenant living in their home.

Tax on rental income FAQs

Do you pay tax on rental income if you have a mortgage?

Yes, you still pay tax on rental income, even if you’re paying off a mortgage.

Having a mortgage on your rental property can significantly affect how much tax you pay.

Previously, landlords could deduct the full amount of mortgage interest from their rental income, but in recent years, tax relief for landlords has been reduced.

Mr Mendes said: “Now, if you let a property, you can only claim a 20pc tax credit on your mortgage interest payments.”

This can mean that landlords who are higher-rate or additional-rate taxpayers will not receive full tax relief on their rental income.

Mr Etherington warned: “In some instances, landlords can make a loss on their rental business but still end up with a tax liability.”

How much can I let my house for without paying taxes?

This depends on your other income. If, as a landlord, you don’t have any other earnings – other than rental income – you might have no income tax to pay. This is because the rent you get may fall within your personal allowance. This allows you to earn a certain amount of income tax-free.

Most individuals get a personal allowance of £12,570 (frozen at this level until April 6 2028) – and will not usually have a tax liability if their taxable income is below this.

Additionally, landlords may benefit from the £1,000 property allowance. This can be used as an alternative to claiming actual expenses if it is more beneficial.

Do I pay tax on rental income if it’s my only income?

Yes. Rental income is taxable, even if it is your only source of income.

That said, as mentioned above, if your total income – including rental income – is below the personal allowance (of £12,570 for the 2025-2026 tax year), you won’t owe any tax.

Mr Mendes said: “Despite this, you are still required to declare the income to HMRC to ensure your records are accurate and up to date. Equally, if your rental income does exceed the personal allowance, then you will be required to pay tax on the amount above the threshold.”

data.title: Sky boss vows to protect news amid funding threat
data.description:
The boss of Sky has vowed to protect the broadcaster’s news business in response to fears about future funding from its US owner.

Dana Strong, the Sky chief executive, told staff during a recent meeting that the media giant would support Sky News for the long term regardless of whether parent company Comcast continues to provide funding.

Comcast guaranteed to keep funding Sky News for 10 years as part of its $39bn (£29bn) takeover of the British broadcaster in 2018.

There have been growing concerns that the US company will pull the plug on the heavily loss-making channel when this commitment expires.

Sky News operates on an annual budget of £100m and is thought to be making losses of as much as £80m. Comcast has already written down its investment in Sky by $8.6bn.

The US conglomerate this week began cutting jobs at NBC News as part of a broader shake-up that will see it spin off its declining cable networks such as MSNBC and CNBC. Sky News last month struck a deal to show its global news coverage on MSNBC.

Sources stressed that Ms Strong’s comments related to Sky’s commitments to its news business, rather than any guarantee of funding from Comcast. The US company has been contacted for comment.

Nevertheless, the assurance is likely to reassure anxious journalists at Sky who have been fearful of job losses.

‘Sky News 2030’

David Rhodes, executive chairman of Sky News, announced a major overhaul of the channel earlier this year in an effort to reduce its reliance on dwindling advertising revenue and generate new sources of income such as subscriptions.

The move will see Sky News focus less on live and breaking news and instead funnel resources into areas such as analysis, as well as podcasts and newsletters.

The shake-up – pointedly dubbed “Sky News 2030” – has already led to several high-profile departures, including long-serving business anchor Ian King and breakfast host Kay Burley, who was part of the original team that launched the channel under Rupert Murdoch in 1989.

Sky News this month launched its new-look flagship breakfast show starring Sophy Ridge and Wilfred Frost, the son of British TV legend Sir David Frost.

Speaking at the industry conference RTS Cambridge last month, Mr Rhodes argued that trusted independent news was not dying but rather “shapeshifting”.

However, he acknowledged that modern audiences were more inclined to trust individual journalists and creators than traditional news brands.

The uncertainty over the future of Sky News comes amid broader job cuts at the broadcaster as it navigates the shift to the streaming era.

Sky, which employs around 23,000 people, last month launched a consultation that is expected to lead to 600 job losses. It is the third round of lay-offs in under two years.

The British media giant is grappling to shore up its position in the market amid a decline in its traditional satellite TV business.

Bosses have outlined plans to scale back investment in new technology after a string of expensive product launches that included the Sky Glass Smart TV and a streaming set-top box.

The company will instead focus on improving its existing products as it battles for subscribers with the likes of Netflix and Disney.

A Sky spokesman said: “We are committed to the long-term success of Sky News. Sky is proud of the award-winning, independent journalism it delivers every day, and of the strong progress being made on the 2030 strategy unveiled earlier this year, which includes investment in digital, video and premium journalism.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Tesco gives delivery drivers body cams after attacks
data.description:
Tesco is giving more than 5,000 delivery drivers body cams to record abusive customers after a wave of attacks on staff.

The supermarket giant said drivers at more than 100 of its stores would be given the cameras by the end of the year, following a surge in threats and violence against them when they make their home deliveries.

According to a recent survey from union Usdaw, more than three quarters of delivery drivers for chains including Asda, Tesco, Sainsbury’s and Morrisons have been abused over the past 12 months, while 13pc have been assaulted.

More than a quarter said they had refused a delivery because they feared for their own personal safety.

Ashwin Prasad, Tesco’s UK chief, said: “Nobody should have to face verbal or physical abuse at work, and the rollout of body-worn cameras will help us protect our drivers, deter would-be offenders and reduce the number of serious incidents.”

He called for ministers to offer new protections to delivery drivers, after they were left out of the Crime and Policing Bill. The bill made it a standalone offence to assault a retail worker, but did not include home delivery drivers.

Mr Prasad said the Government should “send a message to offenders by extending the standalone offence to cover online delivery drivers”.

Daniel Adams, Usdaw’s national officer, said: “No worker should face the risk of threats, abuse and violence in doing their job and we must all do everything that we can to ensure all workplaces are healthy and safe.”

Tesco said a trial of the body cams suggested their use reduced serious incidents against drivers by 50pc.

It comes as Tesco tries new initiatives to clamp down on attacks against its drivers. The Telegraph revealed last year that it had provided delivery drivers with DNA testing kits so that police would be able to trace abusive customers who spat at them.

The so-called “spit kits” were put in drivers’ vans so they could collect samples if customers turned aggressive and spat at them. They were also used during the Covid pandemic.

Tesco has already provided body cams to its shop-floor workers to curb violence against staff within stores. Ken Murphy, Tesco’s chief executive, said he wanted “those who break the law in our stores brought to book”.

According to a survey by the British Retail Consortium (BRC), retail violence and abuse towards shopworkers rose by more than 50pc to over 2,000 incidents per day last year.

In September, Tesco rolled out a new safety app for workers following feedback from staff that they felt unsafe travelling to and from work.

Tesco said workers could choose to download its app, where they could set their destination and how they were travelling. The app would then check in on them during their journeys, so it could raise an SOS alert if they were at risk.

data.title: Everything you need to know about remortgaging your home
data.description:
If your mortgage deal is coming to an end in the next few months, it is time to start the process of remortgaging to switch to a new one.

Getting started early means you’ll avoid ending up on your lender’s standard variable rate, SVR, which is usually much more expensive.

Here, Telegraph Money explains how the remortgaging process works, and how to get the best rate for your mortgage.

  • What is remortgaging?
  • Remortgaging process
  • Where can I find the cheapest interest rates?
  • How long should I lock in a rate for?
  • How can I get the best rate for my mortgage?

What is remortgaging?

Remortgaging refers to taking out a new mortgage deal whenever the fixed rate expires – so, for example, finding a new two-year fix when your current two-year fix runs out.

Technically, you only remortgage if you’re moving to a different lender, but the term can also be used to describe those switching to a different deal with the same lender – but this is actually a “product transfer”.

Why should you remortgage?

There are lots of reasons why you should remortgage, including:

  • Finding a competitive deal – The cheapest rates tend to be on offer from fixed-rate or tracker mortgages, so it’s a good idea to switch straight from one deal to another. Not only will your monthly repayments be lower, you’ll also pay less interest over the course of the mortgage term. You may also save money if your home’s value has increased; the extra equity will mean you could be borrowing a smaller proportion of the total property cost – and a 60pc loan tends to be cheaper than a 75pc loan, for example.
  • Avoiding your lender’s standard variable rate (SVR) – A lender’s SVR is the most expensive option, and can also fluctuate at the lender’s discretion. In general, these rates will go up and down in accordance with the Bank Rate, but they’re not officially linked.
  • Protecting yourself against fluctuations – Opting for a fixed-rate deal means you’ll have the peace of mind of knowing what your repayments will be for the duration of the term. This can be particularly beneficial if mortgage rates rise during the term.
  • Borrowing more – If you want to remove equity from your home, you’ll need to go through the remortgaging process so a lender can decide whether or not to lend you more.

However, there are some instances where it could be worth allowing your mortgage to revert to your lender’s SVR – for example, if you’re planning to move house in the near future and cannot port your mortgage to another property, or if you’re due to finish repaying your mortgage very soon.

In these sorts of cases, the fees you’ll pay for taking out a new deal and possible early repayment charges may not be worth it.

Remortgaging process

1. Start looking around – six months in advance

The remortgaging process can take a while, so if you want to avoid spending time on your lender’s SVR, it’s a good idea to get the process started a few months before your current deal is due to end.

It’s a good idea to make a note of when this will be, but your lender should also get in touch with you well in advance of your deal ending.

You might want to contact a mortgage broker to start looking for deals in advance – some lenders will let you hold a mortgage in principle for up to six months. Though some have cut this back to three or four months.

2. Ask for a closing balance

Also known as a redemption statement, your lender will issue a statement that shows how much is on your remaining mortgage loan, which you’ll need to borrow if you remortgage.

This information will be required when you make an application to a new lender.

3. Decide on a new mortgage

A mortgage broker can help you make this decision, and you might find our guide on whether to choose a fixed-rate or tracker mortgage helpful, along with our mortgage best buys, which show the top rates on offer.

Alternatively, you might want to apply for an interest-only mortgage.

Your broker, if you’re using one, can start the application process once you’ve spotted a deal you want.

4. Instruct a solicitor

This is a necessary step if you’re moving to a different lender. A solicitor or conveyancer will sort the paperwork required for the new contract, such as drawing up the mortgage deeds.

5. Go through affordability checks

Your new lender will need to check that you can afford the mortgage you’re applying for. If you’re simply moving from one repayment mortgage to another, the process should be relatively straightforward, as you’ll likely need to borrow less than you did at the start of your previous deal.

However, if you’re moving to an interest-only deal, or want to remortgage to take equity out of your property, then the affordability checks may be more rigorous.

You’ll likely need some of the following documents:

  • Recent payslips
  • Utility bills
  • Bank statements
  • ID (usually a passport or driving licence)
  • Proof of address.

6. Mortgage valuation

Lenders issue valuations to check the property is worth the amount you want to borrow for it. This is not always done in person – it may be done online, or someone may drive past the property. In some cases, lenders will charge for a valuation to be carried out.

7. Get the mortgage offer

Assuming the previous steps all go to plan, your solicitor should receive a mortgage offer letter. It may contain certain conditions that you’ll have to fulfil when the deal begins (such as paying off remaining credit card debt).

If your circumstances change between receiving the offer and moving to the new mortgage, you should tell your new lender.

8. Move to the new deal

When the time comes your solicitor will be responsible for requesting the money from your new lender, and paying off the debt owing to the old one.

They should also update Land Registry and, in some cases, the title deeds with the new details.

If you’re switching to a new deal with the same lender, some of these steps may be skipped – such as instructing a solicitor.

Where are mortgage rates now?

At the time of writing, the average two-year fixed rate is currently 4.98pc; the average five-year fix is priced slightly more expensive at 5.02pc, according to analyst Moneyfacts.

The average two-year tracker is 4.67pc, while the average SVR is 7.27pc as of October 1.

It’s possible to get much cheaper deals than the average rates, but the cheapest are often reserved for those who are remortgaging and seeking to borrow no more than 60pc of the property’s value.

One decision lots of homeowners have been struggling with is whether to commit to a fixed-rate deal, or opt for a tracker mortgage that’s linked to the Bank Rate. Should interest rates reduce further, those with tracker mortgages will see their mortgage bills get cheaper – until then, you might find yourself on a deal that’s more expensive than the cheapest fixed deals.

Our guide on the fixed rate vs tracker dilemma can help.

What is happening to mortgage rates?

Mortgage rates have broadly on the way down mirroring cuts to the Bank Rate, but have been creeping up more recently as markets price in the fact that we are unlikely to see any further rate cuts this year.

The Bank Rate has been cut three times so far this year, and is currently at 4pc.

Many factors play into the Bank’s decision as to whether to hold, cut or increase the Bank Rate – and other market forces that affect how much lenders charge for borrowing, such as inflation.

The consumer prices index (CPI) measure of inflation measured 3.8pc in September, the same as it was in August. The rate of price growth is significantly above the Government’s target of 2pc.

Rachel Springall, of Moneyfacts.co.uk, said: “Borrowers who are due to come off a cheap fixed rate deal will be on tenterhooks for mortgage rates to drop before they refinance, but if they have some months ahead to wait, it may be wise to consider overpaying.

“The unprecedented volatility in interest rates seen over the past five years alone may persuade borrowers to act now and secure a longer-term fixed deal for peace of mind. As has been the case for two years now, it is cheaper to lock into a five-year fixed mortgage than a two-year deal, based on average rates.”

The next Bank Rate decision is on November 6.

Where can I find the cheapest interest rates?

A mortgage broker can help find the best mortgage deals for you, but it’s also possible to do your own research.

Our guide to the best mortgage rates can help get you started. Its tables are updated every day between Tuesday and Saturday, featuring the top deals for purchase rates and remortgaging, across two, five and 10-year fixed-rates, as well as the best variable-rate options.

It is important to remember that the lowest interest rates do not necessarily equate to the best deal. High fees can sometimes outweigh marginal savings on similarly priced interest rates.

Lenders commonly set fees between £899 and £1,999, but some use percentages instead, such as 0.5pc of the overall loan amount.

Adrian Anderson, of broker Anderson Harris, said: “Don’t just look at the headline rate. Consider the overall cost of the deal, including any fees and whether the lender will pay for a mortgage valuation and legal conveyance, which most banks do.”

If you are locking in for longer than two years then be sure to check any repayment penalties which would apply should your circumstances change and you need to exit the deal early – these can run into the thousands of pounds.

Our mortgage increase calculator can show how much your bills will change as a result of remortgaging to a new deal.

How long should I lock in a rate for?

Thousands of today’s homeowners with fixed mortgages are still on rates agreed when the Bank Rate was far lower than it is today. This means they are likely to face a steep increase in their payments when they reach the end of their current deal and take out a new loan.

This is because while fixed rates may be falling, the cost of borrowing is still inflated when compared with mortgages which were locked in two or five years ago. Borrowers coming off those deals and searching for a new rate are very likely to face higher rates.

Brokers have been suggesting a typical borrower should fix for two years as this would minimise the amount of time spent fixed on an inflated rate.

Nicholas Mendes, of broker John Charcol, said: “For borrowers evaluating their options, the choice between fixed and variable rates remains key. A fixed-rate mortgage provides stability, locking in payments for a set period, whereas a variable-rate mortgage tracks the Bank of England’s base rate, offering potential savings if rates decline further.

“For those comfortable with a slightly higher initial rate, a shorter-term fixed deal or a tracker mortgage offers flexibility, allowing a switch should rates fall further. Conversely, a longer-term fix provides certainty and protection against potential market volatility.

“Whether to wait or lock in a deal now depends on personal financial circumstances, risk tolerance, and market conditions. Seeking professional mortgage advice is crucial to making an informed decision.”

How can I get the best rate for my mortgage?

Increase your equity

Borrowers who put more equity into a property make themselves less risky customers, increasing the chances a lender will be happier to offer them lower interest rates. Banks use the term “loan-to-value ratio” (LTV) to label how much they lend a borrower against a home.

If you have a repayment mortgage, you’ll naturally increase your equity over time, but if you want to speed up the process then you can consider overpaying your mortgage.

For example, a £160,000 mortgage on a £200,000 home would be a loan-to-value of 80pc. A lower loan-to-value and bigger deposit will usually unlock lower interest rates. Mr Anderson said: “It can make quite a big difference to the amount of interest you pay over the term of the deal. So if you have cash available you may want to consider paying down part of the mortgage to access a better rate.”

Our mortgage overpayment calculator can help you weigh up whether you’re better off overpaying your mortgage, or putting your extra cash into a savings account.

Consider an offset mortgage

Households with a significant cash pile could also access lower rates by using an offset mortgage. A handful of banks allow borrowers to reduce the cost of their loan using cash held in an account with the same lender. For example, a customer borrowing a £500,000 mortgage and with £200,000 in savings would only pay interest on £300,000 of the loan, but will forfeit any interest on the cash pot.

Based on a mortgage interest rate of 4.5pc, this would reduce monthly interest from £1,873 a month to £1,124 – a saving of £749 each month.

Mr Anderson said: “Offset mortgages are proving especially popular as tax thresholds are shrinking and reducing households’ personal allowances.

“There should be no tax to pay on savings used to offset the mortgage balance and you should not be paying interest on the mortgage balance offset by the cash funds. The account should also be instant access so you still have access to liquidity if circumstances change.”

Borrowers opting for a more specialist mortgage, such as an offset deal, should consult a mortgage adviser.

What about interest-only mortgages?

Any homeowners struggling to pay their mortgage bills are able to switch to interest-only deals without a formal repayment plan. City watchdog, the Financial Conduct Authority, announced the change last year in a bid to help lenders provide mortgage forbearance at scale.

However, once the temporary interest-free period is over, homeowners must make up their repayments. To switch to a permanent interest-only deal, you’ll still need a credible repayment plan.

data.title: The painful hidden losses looming for taxpayers on broadband
data.description:
After pumping yet more public money into CityFibre over the summer in the form of loans, the National Wealth Fund (NWF) was ebullient about the prospects for the challenger broadband provider.

“CityFibre’s investment marks a significant moment in upgrading the UK’s digital infrastructure to help provide the digital foundations for the UK’s economic growth for decades to come,” said the body, which reports to Rachel Reeves.

The borrowing from the taxpayer provided a vital lifeline for CityFibre, which had been locked in funding discussions with investors for the best part of the year after warning it was about to run out of cash.

The backing represents just a small proportion of the total taxpayer money that has funnelled into “alt-net” broadband firms, who are rolling out full-fibre networks to challenge BT.

But now, with the financial outlook for alt-nets darkening, private lenders are bracing for heavy losses on their broadband investments, raising the prospect that large sums of taxpayer money could also go down the drain.

The £7.3bn NWF was established last year by the Chancellor with a brief to encourage private sector investment into major infrastructure projects across the UK. It was later combined with the UK Infrastructure Bank, giving the body a total pool of £27.8bn to invest.

The fund has wasted no time in splashing the cash. The Telegraph has learnt that the NWF contributed at least £90m in debt to CityFibre’s latest fundraising in July.

That accounts for almost 10pc of the total new debt raised – a disproportionate sum in comparison with other lenders, which include Lloyds and NatWest.

The latest tranche of loans builds on £200m of taxpayer money provided in a previous financing round in 2022, making the total taxpayer funding available to CityFibre around £300m.

Court documents show CityFibre has drawn down £142.8m of that first tranche – the maximum it was entitled to after failing to meet geographic coverage conditions under a “drawstop” clause. That debt is now junior to the new investment, meaning it ranks lower in priority for repayment and at a higher risk of losses.

The NWF’s enthusiasm for supporting the troubled sector extends beyond CityFibre, however. In June, it handed £50m to Dorset-based Wessex Internet. 

Other recent deals include a £75m loan to Netomnia and £250m for Nexfibre, whose network is used by Virgin Media O2. Overall, the NWF has invested around £1.65bn into the broadband sector.

This despite a far from rosy outlook for the investors who have backed alt-nets. Surging interest rates have pushed up the cost of building new fibre networks to eye-watering levels. Incumbent BT, meanwhile, has proved more resilient to new challengers than expected and signing up customers has been a costly and difficult business.

As a result, many alt-nets are generating revenues that barely cover their interest payments, let alone their operating costs.

Across the sector, these upstart broadband firms have begun to slow down or even halt their network build as they seek to shore up their finances. The industry is now gearing up for a wave of consolidation, but with many waiting for others to make the first move at the behest of investors unwilling to admit large chunks of their outlay need to be written off to make deals work.

CityFibre’s refinancing came after an almost year-long stand-off between lenders and shareholders over who would pump in more money. Bosses had warned that funding would run out in mid-2025 “in all scenarios”. 

Newlight Partners and Patrizia, which became minority shareholders in CityFibre following the company’s acquisitions of Lit Fibre and Connexin, did not take part in the refinancing.

Hyperoptic, which specialises in installing full-fibre broadband in blocks of flats, lost another £144m in 2024, with bosses warning of a “material uncertainty” over its ability to continue without fresh funding. London-focused alt-net G Network has been seeking a buyer for more than a year, while rural firm Gigaclear is also on the hunt for more funding.

But with the prospect of returns diminishing, lenders are starting to get jitters. Lloyds said over the summer that it had set aside £25m to cover loans that will not be repaid. Its investments in the sector include CityFibre and Gigaclear, which are both also backed by the taxpayer.

NatWest, another CityFibre backer and which is estimated to have lent around £1bn to alt-nets, is said to have also readied its accounts for pain.

“It’s a prudent reflection of the fact that there are companies out there that are probably not going to last the course,” says Philip Carse, chief analyst at Megabuyte.

“Valuations are well below invested capital so on the face of it a lot of people are going to end up not getting any sort of equity return.”

With a wave of consolidation looming, writedowns will in part be disguised through opaque debt-for-equity swaps, where lenders to the target alt-net have their debt converted into loans to the acquirer. Ultimately, it may take a major acquisition for cash to force the industry to “mark to market” and crystallise big losses.

Nevertheless, the wider malaise in the industry raises the prospect that the NWF will have to take significant writedowns on its investments – meaning major losses for the taxpayer.

In its latest available accounts to March 2024, the NWF booked an impairment of £46m, “primarily reflecting the challenging funding environment in the UK’s fibre broadband sector during the financial year”.

John Flint, the NWF chief executive, added: “We have had to continue to provide significant support to the digital sector in the face of liquidity challenges and a lack of appetite from the private markets.”

Further writedowns are feared when its latest accounts are published in the coming weeks.

The NWF says its purpose is to take risks and drive investment in areas that the private sector would otherwise not touch. Its strategy is based on the “crowding-in” principle, where state backing encourages private investment by reducing the risk.

Part of the ‘machine’

The Government separately supports broadband firms through Building Digital UK (BDUK), which provides taxpayer subsidies for companies to build their networks in the most rural areas that would not otherwise be commercially viable. As of August, there were active contracts totalling £2.4bn under this scheme.

Carse insists the NWF has done a good job overall, describing it as a “necessary part of the machine” alongside BDUK. “It genuinely filled a gap over the last one or two years when the private sector has pulled back,” he adds.

But others fear that the NWF has now strayed away from its core principles. Analysts question why the fund has invested in companies such as Netomnia and Hyperoptic, which are well-established and building in areas that risk being quickly overbuilt by BT’s Openreach.

Karen Egan, at Enders Analysis, says: “The additional network choice is not worthwhile from a policy perspective in my view and it also makes it considerably more likely that that alt-net will never make any money.”

Moreover, the NWF’s readiness to keep ploughing money into the sector even as private investors retreat has raised questions over the Government’s investment strategy.

“When the private sector has been funding a sector, but starts to retreat as the economics are not stacking up as originally hoped, it doesn’t seem like good use of taxpayer’s money to prop it up,” says Egan.

“We are supposedly a free market economy, after all – and if the markets are telling you that there isn’t space for three separate fibre networks in an area then it’s probably quite right about that.”

The ongoing investment of public money into alt-nets has fuelled speculation that Labour is simply not willing to let any businesses fail under its watch – even if that means very little of its investment will be clawed back.

But with broadband bosses braced for a looming shake-out, it’s likely the taxpayer will ultimately be on the hook.

A spokesman for the NFW said: “Ensuring good digital connectivity across the UK is key to growth – an essential part of the NWF’s mission – so it is unsurprising we have been active in this sector.

“We only commit capital where we are needed and, in the case of alt-nets where market appetite is restricted, we have worked to crowd in commercial investors to help meet the Government’s connectivity ambitions – thereby sharing the risk with the private sector.”

The spokesman added: “We, like commercial investors, go into every transaction seeking to make a return in the long-term and we have crowded in over £16bn across all our sectors, including digital, over the last four years – that’s private investment that would not have otherwise been there.”

CityFibre declined to comment.

data.title: Netflix ‘plotting Warner Bros takeover’
data.description:
Netflix is said to be considering a bid for Warner Bros Discovery (WBD) after the Hollywood conglomerate said it was open to takeover offers.

The streaming giant is reportedly among suitors interested in WBD, which is behind Harry Potter and owner of channels including HBO and CNN. Comcast, Sky’s US owner, has also been named as a potential bidder by CNBC.

A takeover of Warner Bros would mark an audacious swoop by Netflix, giving the company access to a rich back catalogue of blockbusters ranging from The Lord of the Rings to Barbie, as well as TV hits including Friends and Succession.

Any deal would mark the latest incursion of Silicon Valley into Hollywood after Amazon bought MGM, the studio behind the James Bond franchise, in an $8.5bn (£6.3bn) deal in 2022.

WBD has already been fielding takeover interest from the billionaire Ellison family, who aim to combine it with Hollywood rival Paramount.

The Ellisons already control Paramount in a consortium alongside RedBird Capital Partners, the private equity firm that is also seeking ownership of The Telegraph.

However, WBD reportedly rejected an approach from the Ellisons’ Paramount Skydance for being too low.

WBD on Tuesday said it was open to a sale after receiving “unsolicited interest” from multiple suitors, firing the starting gun on a potential bidding war.

The media giant is currently planning to split its streaming and studios division from its traditional TV networks, but will now expand its strategic review to consider a sale of either parts of the group or the whole business.

It is thought that Netflix and Comcast would be more interested in WBD’s film and TV studios business than its legacy TV networks. Both companies declined to comment.

David Zaslav, chief executive of WBD, said: “It’s no surprise that the significant value of our portfolio is receiving increased recognition by others in the market.

“After receiving interest from multiple parties, we have initiated a comprehensive review of strategic alternatives to identify the best path forward to unlock the full value of our assets.”

Shares in the Hollywood giant jumped by more than 11pc in New York.

The comments signal a change in direction for WBD, which had previously been focused on completing its planned spin-off by mid-2026.

The prospect of a bidding war will mark a setback to Paramount Skydance, which is led by David Ellison. It was formed over the summer through the $8bn takeover of Paramount, the maker of blockbusters including Titanic and Forrest Gump and owner of British broadcaster Channel 5.

A tie-up between Paramount and WBD would represent a major reshaping of the US media landscape. Skydance hopes a deal would give the combined entity the scale required to take on the might of deep-pocketed rivals such as Netflix and Disney.

WBD was itself formed just three years ago in a $43bn merger. However, it has struggled to fend off fierce competition from streaming rivals and stem declining audiences in its traditional cable TV business.

The company’s share price has languished as it also struggled under its huge debt pile, which stood at $35.6bn at the end of June. However, shares have recovered almost 60pc since news of Skydance’s interest first emerged.

The looming sale process will be a key test for Mr Zaslav, a veteran media executive who previously led Discovery before taking the top job at WBD following the merger.

Mr Zaslav has faced criticisms for decisions made under his tenure, including cancelling projects such as the DC film Batgirl and carrying out a botched rebrand of the HBO Max streaming service.

He also faced a shareholder backlash over his $52m pay package last year, which came amid job cuts at the company’s film studios.

Skydance’s interest in WBD comes against a backdrop of broader empire-building by the Ellisons. Larry Ellison, David’s billionaire father, is part of a consortium of buyers lined up to take control of TikTok’s US operations.

Shortly after the Paramount takeover, Skydance expanded its grip on US media by buying The Free Press, an online news website founded by outspoken journalist Bari Weiss, for a reported $150m.

Larry Ellison, who is a close friend of Sir Tony Blair and has donated hundreds of millions of dollars to the former prime minister’s non-profit institute, has also pledged to invest $5bn in data centres across the UK and has set up a major new research centre in Oxford.

Skydance’s bid for Paramount was backed by RedBird Capital, the US private equity firm trying to take control of the Telegraph.

RedBird earlier this month asked ministers to begin formal consideration of whether its takeover can go ahead.

Netflix’s third quarter earnings on Tuesday showed its revenue rose by 17pc year-on-year to $11.5bn, a jump that was driven by membership growth, price rises and record advertising sales.

However, the streaming company’s net income rose to just $2.5bn, below the $2.98bn it had expected. Its operating margin of 28pc was also less than it had forecast due to a tax dispute in Brazil.

data.title: Thames Water crisis threatens London super-sewer
data.description:
Thames Water’s debt crisis threatens to trigger millions of pounds in losses for London’s £4.5bn super-sewer.

Tideway Tunnel, which owns the project, has sounded the alarm ahead of a looming handover later this year – with bosses fearing Thames could withhold cash in its scramble to avoid nationalisation.

The sewer owner said it is closely monitoring Thames’s plight and has sought assurances from the Government to ensure the project will not be threatened if the company collapses into special administration.

In its latest annual report, Tideway said: “We continue to note Thames Water’s evolving financial position and this has led to concern that their situation might change.

“There is a possible risk that Thames Water, given its current financial position, or a special administrator may choose not to pay an element of Tideway’s revenue.

“This would not be consistent with Thames Water’s licence and would be expected to lead to a breach and enforcement action.”

As part of its contingency plans, Tideway also considered the possibility of Thames failing to support the sewer’s operations, warning that this “could affect our ability to deliver our investment programme on time and on budget.

“If Thames Water does not comply with the revenue agreement, it could have a financial impact,” the company added.

Concerns over Thames Water’s future come as the company struggles under the weight of a £17bn debt pile.

The company admitted last month that it could enter a taxpayer-backed special administration regime if a rescue deal led by more than 100 creditors fails to succeed.

Thames’s financial crisis is in stark contrast to the completion of Tideway, which has been hailed as a rare infrastructure success story.

The 25km tunnel under the River Thames, which became fully operational earlier this year, represents the biggest improvement to London’s sewage network in 150 years.

Buried 67m deep, the concrete pipe is designed to ease pollution pressures by redirecting sewage away from the river and into a treatment works in east London.

While Thames will not own Tideway, it is expected to be charged an annual fee for its use of the sewer. This will be paid to Bazalgette, a company bankrolled by a group of banks and pension funds.

A Tideway spokesman said: “Tideway is a completely separate, independent company set up to deliver the Thames Tideway Tunnel project.

“This vital new infrastructure is now operational, and has already diverted away nearly eight million tonnes of sewage that would have historically spilled into the Thames.

“We remain confident that Tideway’s revenues are well protected given water industry legislation and the licence obligations that apply to both Thames Water and Tideway. We continue to work effectively with Thames Water to deliver the benefits of the project.”

A Thames spokesman said: “Thames Water collects revenue from customers on Tideway’s behalf and passes it on to Tideway on receipt. Tideway’s revenues are well protected given water industry legislation and the license obligations that apply to both Thames Water and Tideway. We continue to work effectively with Tideway to deliver the benefits of the project.”

data.title: Europe putting vital medicines at risk, warns Ratcliffe’s Ineos
data.description:
One of the last factories in Europe that makes a key chemical for statins, antibiotics and vitamins is at risk of closure amid a flood of cheap foreign imports, Sir Jim Ratcliffe’s Ineos has warned.

The chemicals group has said its plant in Mark, Germany had been put at risk because of “dumping” by Chinese rivals.

The loss of the site risked leaving health services dependent on “unreliable foreign supply chains”, Sir Jim’s company said.

It is the latest warning of the threat posed to European industry by China, where domestic supply gluts in goods such as steel and chemicals is driving firms to export huge amounts goods at rock-bottom prices.

Critics say this excess capacity is a result of heavy state support and in some cases lower environmental standards.

Ineos blamed Chinese dumping for a collapse in European production of a chemical known as 1,4-Butanediol (BDO), which used to make medicines such as antibiotics, statins and Vitamin B6.

The company said the market had become “dominated” by imports from Xinjiang, China, which were “made using energy from coal, then shipped halfway around the world and dumped in Europe at dirt-cheap, rock-bottom prices”, creating an “enormous” carbon footprint.

By comparison, Ineos said its German plant was “one of the cleanest in the world” but was still hit by “sky high European carbon taxes, which no one else in the world has to pay”.

The company added: “Without urgent support from government and the EU Commission to block unfair imports, this site and its 200 jobs have no future.

“Once gone, the capacity to manufacture critical pharmaceutical products in Europe will disappear, leaving patients reliant on unreliable foreign supply chains of unknown quality.

“In the worst case, life-saving medicines could simply become unavailable.”

The European Union is preparing to impose new rules next year in a bid to level the playing field between domestic and foreign goods when it comes to carbon emissions.

It comes after years of complaints by European firms that they are forced to pay higher taxes and switch to green energy supplies, while foreign firms relying on dirtier forms of power such as coal undercut them with cheap prices.

Andrew Brown, chief executive of Ineos Enterprises, added: “Urgent action is needed. Whilst China and the US defend and subsidise their industries, Europe buries its own with sky-high energy costs, carbon taxes and suffocating bureaucracy.

“Europe cannot compete against state-backed imports without swift, strong, trade barriers.

“Failure to act will destroy Europe’s ability to produce many of its own medicines, putting basic healthcare at risk.”
data.author: The telegraph
